proposals.title: A Single-Cell Functional Atlas of Apoptotic Commitment Reveals Context-Dependent Reversibility and Bypass
proposals.motivation: The canonical model of apoptosis presents a linear, irreversible cascade, yet clinical and experimental observations suggest significant heterogeneity in cell death commitment. In cancer and autoimmunity, cells exhibit unexpected survival despite death receptor engagement or caspase activation, implying context-dependent logic gates and escape routes that are not captured by population-level assays. A systematic, high-resolution map of this decision landscape is critically needed to understand therapeutic resistance and aberrant survival.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the commitment point in apoptosis is not fixed but is a variable, context-dependent threshold influenced by cell type, metabolic state, and stimulus, allowing for phenotypic reversal or pathway bypass under specific conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ a multiplexed single-cell functional genomics platform, combining CRISPRi/a perturbations of core nodes (e.g., FADD, caspase-8, Apaf-1, XIAP, Bcl-2) with real-time imaging of FRET-based caspase-3/8 activity and mitochondrial potential in diverse primary human cell types (T cells, hepatocytes, epithelial cells). Cells will be exposed to a panel of death stimuli (FasL, TNF, staurosporine, etoposide). Commitment dynamics will be analyzed using pre-2026 trajectory inference algorithms (e.g., Slingshot, PAGA) and hidden Markov models to define probabilistic state transitions from initiation to irreversible execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first predictive, context-resolved map of apoptotic commitment, identifying specific cellular conditions that permit feedback inhibition or alternative survival signaling. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding heterogeneous treatment responses in patients and may reveal new therapeutic targets to modulate cell fate in disease.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond testing pathway necessity to dynamically quantify the sufficiency and reversibility of apoptotic signals across a multidimensional space of genetic perturbations and cellular contexts, a systematic approach not previously undertaken.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Irreversibility of Apoptosis: An Integrated Experimental and Computational Search for Feedback and Reversal Points
proposals.motivation: The canonical view of apoptosis as a linear, irreversible cascade is fundamental to cell biology and therapeutic design. However, emerging observations of 'aborted apoptosis' and cellular recovery after mitochondrial permeabilization challenge this dogma, suggesting potential regulatory feedback. Understanding if and how apoptosis can be modulated or reversed has profound implications for treating diseases of dysregulated cell death, such as cancer and neurodegeneration.
proposals.hypothesis: The apoptotic cascade, particularly downstream of mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP), contains latent feedback mechanisms and points of reversibility that can be revealed by targeted perturbations, making it a regulable cybernetic system rather than a simple domino effect.
proposals.method: We will use genetically engineered human cell lines with inducible, fluorescent reporters for caspase-3/7 activity and mitochondrial membrane potential. Apoptosis will be initiated via inducible expression of FasL or BH3-only proteins. At defined stages post-MOMP, we will perturb the system using pan-caspase inhibitors (e.g., z-VAD-fmk), pro-survival signal surges (e.g., acute IGF-1 stimulation), or agents promoting mitochondrial recovery. Cell fate (recovery, delayed death) will be tracked by live-cell imaging and flow cytometry. In parallel, we will construct a detailed ordinary differential equation (ODE) model of the core apoptotic pathway, incorporating known inhibitors (e.g., XIAP, Bcl-2). Using parameter estimation from our experimental data and sensitivity analysis, we will computationally screen for potential, yet uncharacterized, negative feedback loops that could confer bistability or reversibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of potential reversal points in the intrinsic and extrinsic apoptotic pathways. It will generate a predictive computational model that identifies candidate molecules for feedback regulation. The findings could redefine the conceptual framework of apoptosis, opening new avenues for therapeutic intervention aimed at fine-tuning cell death commitment.
proposals.novelty: While apoptosis regulation is well-studied, directly testing its irreversibility as an engineering control problem is novel. The integrated approach of applying precise temporal perturbations with computational modeling to uncover latent feedback moves beyond descriptive studies of known inhibitors.

proposals.title: Chromatin Catabolites as Active DAMPs: A Novel Signaling Role for DNA Fragmentation in Apoptosis and Tissue Homeostasis
proposals.motivation: Apoptotic DNA fragmentation is considered a passive endpoint of cell death, crucial for genomic cleanup. However, the immunological Danger Signal Theory posits that endogenous damage-derived molecules can act as active signals (DAMPs). It remains unknown whether specific chromatin degradation products generated during apoptosis, a process central to Nagata's work on FasL and caspase-activated DNase, serve as active signaling entities to coordinate tissue-level responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific chromatin catabolites, such as nucleosome-sized DNA fragments or oligonucleotides with defined modifications (e.g., oxidized bases), generated during apoptosis are not merely waste but act as DAMPs by binding to pattern recognition receptors (e.g., TLR9, AIM2) on neighboring cells, thereby actively transmitting information about cell death to shape tissue repair and immune responses.
proposals.method: We will use established mouse models of FasL-induced apoptosis and conditional knockout mice lacking apoptotic nucleases (e.g., CAD-deficient). Chromatin fragments released from dying cells will be characterized by size fractionation and mass spectrometry. In vitro, purified fragments will be tested for their ability to activate reporter cells expressing TLR9, AIM2, or other candidate receptors. In vivo, the functional impact of blocking specific DAMP-receptor interactions (using neutralizing antibodies or receptor antagonists) on post-apoptotic immune cell recruitment, compensatory proliferation, and fibrosis will be assessed in tissue injury models.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a paradigm-shifting concept: that apoptotic DNA breakdown is an active communicative event. It will identify novel DAMP molecules derived from chromatin and define their receptor-mediated signaling pathways. This could reveal new therapeutic targets for modulating sterile inflammation, tissue repair, and fibrotic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While DAMPs from necrosis are known, the idea that the structured, enzymatic process of apoptotic DNA fragmentation produces specific, active signaling molecules is novel. It challenges the canonical view of apoptosis as immunologically silent and re-frames a terminal biochemical event as a source of regulatory information.

proposals.title: Synthetic Reconstitution of Apoptotic Logic: A Modular Framework to Decode Tissue-Specific Fas Signaling Fates
proposals.motivation: Despite the established role of Fas-mediated apoptosis in immune homeostasis and disease, the molecular logic governing why specific tissues are exquisitely sensitive or profoundly resistant remains unclear. This gap hinders our ability to predict pathological outcomes, such as in autoimmune attack or immune evasion by tumors, and to rationally design therapies that modulate this pathway. Current observational and correlative studies cannot establish causality between regulatory mechanisms and cellular fate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tissue-specific susceptibility to Fas-mediated apoptosis is governed by a definable, modular logic of pathway components and inhibitors, which can be synthetically reconstituted to predictably switch a cell's apoptotic fate.
proposals.method: We will engineer a modular, synthetic Fas signaling system in human cell lines from sensitive (e.g., activated lymphocytes) and resistant (e.g., hepatocytes, certain carcinomas) tissues. Using CRISPR-Cas9 and inducible expression systems, we will create a core chassis with endogenous Fas receptor and key adaptors (FADD) knocked out, then reintroduce them under exogenous control. We will systematically test candidate resistance modules—such as overexpression of c-FLIP isoforms, Bcl-2 family proteins, or inhibitor of caspase-activated DNase (ICAD)—in sensitive cells, and conversely, use shRNA or dominant-negative constructs to disable them in resistant cells. Apoptotic output will be quantified via real-time caspase-8/3 activity assays and DNA fragmentation analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal, design-based framework for apoptotic regulation, moving from correlation to engineering principles. It will define the sufficiency and necessity of specific regulatory nodes, providing a predictive model for tissue fate. The resulting modular platform will serve as a foundational tool for testing therapeutic strategies aimed at sensitizing resistant tumors or protecting tissues in autoimmune contexts.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying synthetic biology and engineering principles to deconstruct the apoptotic signaling pathway, allowing for the first time a systematic, causal test of regulatory logic rather than observational association. It directly addresses the long-standing puzzle of tissue-specific fate determination through controlled reconstitution.

proposals.title: Live-cell kinetic profiling of the Fas DISC reveals tissue-specific apoptotic signaling architectures in primary human B cells
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand-induced apoptosis is crucial for immune homeostasis and tolerance, but its dysregulation contributes to autoimmune disease and cancer. While the core pathway is known, how tissue microenvironment influences the real-time dynamics and efficiency of apoptotic signaling in primary human immune cells remains a major unresolved question. Current endpoint assays cannot capture the kinetic heterogeneity that may underlie differential cellular sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that primary B cells from distinct human lymphoid tissues possess unique kinetic architectures of the Fas signaling network, defined by differential rates of DISC assembly and caspase-8 activation, which dictate their apoptotic fate.
proposals.method: We will engineer a panel of multiplexed FRET biosensors targeting key Fas pathway nodes: Fas receptor oligomerization, FADD recruitment, and caspase-8 cleavage. These biosensors will be expressed via optimized lentiviral transduction in primary human B cells isolated from spleen, lymph nodes, and peripheral blood of patient samples. Using live-cell spinning-disk confocal microscopy, we will simultaneously monitor multiple FRET signals in single cells following stimulation with recombinant human FasL, correlating real-time interaction kinetics with cell death outcomes measured by Annexin V and propidium iodide.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first live-cell kinetic map of the intrinsic Fas pathway in primary human B cells, directly linking signaling dynamics to cell fate. It will identify which kinetic parameters (e.g., delay time, activation rate) are predictive of tissue-specific apoptotic sensitivity, providing a new functional dimension to understanding immune regulation and pathology.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of multiplexed live-cell FRET biosensors with primary human B cells from defined tissue compartments allows for the direct observation of tissue-specific signaling kinetics, moving beyond static biochemical analysis of cell lines or mixed populations.

proposals.title: Systematic characterization of hyper-proliferative and inflammatory phenotypes in human immune cells harboring defined apoptotic signaling mutations
proposals.motivation: Loss-of-function mutations in core apoptotic molecules like Fas, caspase-8, or FADD are linked to autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS) and other immune disorders, but the precise cellular mechanisms driving lymphopliferation and autoimmunity remain incompletely defined. A comprehensive, side-by-side analysis of the consequences of disrupting different nodes in the extrinsic and intrinsic apoptotic pathways in primary human cells is lacking, limiting our understanding of disease heterogeneity and potential therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct loss-of-function mutations in key apoptotic signaling molecules (e.g., Fas, FADD, caspase-8, Apaf-1) will induce unique hyper-proliferative and inflammatory transcriptional programs in primary human T cells, driven not only by failed cell death but also by dysregulated non-apoptotic signaling pathways.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR-Cas9 to introduce specific, patient-derived loss-of-function mutations into genes encoding Fas, FADD, caspase-8, and Apaf-1 in primary human CD4+ T cells from healthy donors. Isogenic mutant and control cells will be subjected to detailed phenotypic analysis: proliferation assays (CFSE dilution), cytokine profiling (multiplex ELISA), and RNA sequencing to define transcriptomic signatures. We will specifically test for constitutive NF-κB or MAPK activation (via phospho-flow cytometry) in these mutants, assessing known non-apoptotic roles of molecules like caspase-8. Responses to re-stimulation via the T-cell receptor and sensitivity to FasL-induced apoptosis will be compared across genotypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive functional map linking specific apoptotic gene defects to distinct cellular hyperactivity profiles, moving beyond simple survival assays. It will clarify the contribution of aberrant non-apoptotic signaling to disease phenotypes. The data will provide a mechanistic framework for stratifying patients with apoptotic defects and identifying signaling nodes for targeted pharmacological intervention.
proposals.novelty: While the role of these molecules in apoptosis is well-established, systematically comparing their non-apoptotic, pro-proliferative functions in isogenic primary human T cells—particularly the potential for caspase-8 mutants to drive inflammation independent of Apaf-1—is a novel and direct experimental approach to deciphering ALPS-like pathophysiology.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic and Proteomic Profiling Reveals Determinants of Heterogeneous Apoptotic Response to Fas Ligand
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL)-induced apoptosis is a critical mechanism in immune regulation and tissue homeostasis, yet population-level studies mask significant cell-to-cell variability in response timing and resistance. This heterogeneity can undermine therapeutic efficacy in cancer or contribute to autoimmune pathology. Understanding its molecular basis, using pre-2026 single-cell technologies, is essential to predict and modulate cell fate decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-existing, stochastic heterogeneity in the expression levels of components within the Fas signaling pathway (e.g., Fas receptor, caspase-8, c-FLIP, anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 proteins) and in metabolic state determines the timing and probability of an individual cell undergoing apoptosis upon FasL engagement.
proposals.method: We will use primary human T cells and established lymphoid cell lines. Cells will be exposed to a uniform dose of recombinant human FasL, and apoptotic progression will be tracked in real-time using live-cell imaging with fluorescent caspase-3/7 reporters. At defined timepoints (pre-stimulation, early, and late apoptosis), single cells will be captured using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform for parallel single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and CITE-seq (Cellular Indexing of Transcriptomes and Epitopes by Sequencing) with antibody-derived tags (ADTs) against key proteins (Fas, caspase-8, c-FLIP). Bioinformatic analysis will employ Seurat for clustering and differential expression, and will build predictive models using random forest regression to correlate pre-stimulation expression signatures with subsequent apoptotic kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a high-resolution map of the molecular states that confer resistance or sensitivity to FasL at the single-cell level. It will identify key biomarker combinations that predict apoptotic fate, moving beyond population averages. The findings will provide a foundational framework for understanding fractional killing in immune responses and for developing strategies to overcome apoptosis resistance.
proposals.novelty: While Nagata's lab has defined core Fas pathway components, applying integrated single-cell transcriptomics, surface proteomics, and live-cell kinetics to this classic apoptosis model to deconvolve pre-existing heterogeneity is a novel synthesis. This approach moves from a deterministic pathway model to a probabilistic, single-cell fate model.

proposals.title: Reconstitution of a Minimal Apoptotic Machinery: Defining the Sufficiency of Cytosolic Factors for Caspase Activation and DNA Fragmentation
proposals.motivation: Apoptosis is executed by caspases and caspase-activated DNase (CAD), but the essential cellular compartment for this process remains ambiguous. While mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP) and ER stress are well-documented initiators, Nagata's prior work on caspase-8-independent pathways and CAD regulation suggests the existence of a core cytosolic death program. Determining if cytosolic factors alone are sufficient would fundamentally redefine the minimal requirements for apoptosis, distinguishing core executioners from regulatory or amplifying organelle-derived signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a defined set of purified cytosolic proteins, including key caspases (e.g., caspase-3, -7), CAD, its inhibitor (ICAD), and necessary co-factors, is sufficient to recapitulate the hallmark biochemical events of apoptosis—specifically, caspase activation and internucleosomal DNA fragmentation—in a cell-free system devoid of intact membranes and organelles.
proposals.method: We will establish a refined cell-free system using cytosolic extracts from human cells (e.g., Jurkat) rigorously depleted of membrane and organellar components by ultracentrifugation and density gradient fractionation. Apoptosis will be initiated by adding recombinant, constitutively active upstream signals (e.g., active caspase-8 or tBid). We will assay for caspase-3/7 activity fluorometrically and monitor DNA fragmentation using a plasmid nicking assay and gel electrophoresis for nucleosomal laddering. The sufficiency of purified components will be tested by reconstituting the pathway from recombinant human proteins (caspase-3, CAD, ICAD, etc.) in a defined buffer.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the minimal biochemical requirements for the execution phase of apoptosis, potentially identifying a 'cytosolic apoptosome' core. It will clarify whether organellar components are strictly required or primarily serve as signal integration hubs. The findings will provide a purified system for screening novel modulators of the core death machinery, with implications for understanding pathological cell death.
proposals.novelty: While cell-free systems exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on rigorously excluding all membrane/organelle contributions to test the sufficiency of a defined cytosolic proteome, moving beyond correlative evidence from inhibitor studies. It directly challenges the presumed obligatory role of mitochondrial factors in all apoptotic paradigms, building on Nagata's expertise in caspase-independent and CAD-mediated death.

proposals.title: Purification and Functional Characterization of Cytosolic Factor(s) Mediating Caspase-Independent Nuclear Apoptosis
proposals.motivation: Classical apoptosis requires caspase activation, but our lab and others have identified caspase-independent pathways, such as those triggered by FasL, where cytosolic extracts induce nuclear apoptosis (DNA fragmentation, chromatin condensation). The specific cytosolic factor(s) responsible for this activity remain unidentified, representing a critical gap in understanding the full apoptotic machinery. Identifying these factors is essential for comprehending cell death in contexts where caspases are inhibited, such as in certain cancers or during viral infection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cytosolic extract from FasL-stimulated cells contains one or more specific, non-caspase proteases or nucleases that are necessary and sufficient to induce nuclear apoptosis in a cell-free system.
proposals.method: We will use a biochemical fractionation approach. Cytosolic extracts from human Jurkat T cells treated with anti-Fas antibody will be subjected to sequential chromatography (ion-exchange, size-exclusion, and hydrophobic interaction). Apoptosis-inducing activity will be tracked using a cell-free assay with isolated HeLa cell nuclei, monitoring DNA fragmentation and morphology. Active fractions will be analyzed by mass spectrometry to identify candidate proteins. Candidate function will be validated using specific inhibitors, siRNA knockdown in donor cells, and recombinant protein reconstitution assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively identify the key cytosolic executor(s) of caspase-independent nuclear apoptosis, establishing a new molecular pathway for programmed cell death. It will provide novel targets for therapeutic intervention in diseases with dysregulated apoptosis and clarify the relationship between caspase-dependent and -independent death signals.
proposals.novelty: While the phenomenon of caspase-independent, cytosolic factor-mediated apoptosis is known, the specific identity of the active molecule(s) is unresolved. This proposal moves beyond phenomenological description to direct biochemical isolation and characterization, a logical but technically challenging next step from our prior work on Apaf-1/caspase-8-independent pathways.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Validation of the Critical ICE-like Protease Substrate Responsible for Initiating Apoptotic DNA Fragmentation
proposals.motivation: The execution phase of apoptosis is driven by the proteolytic activity of caspases (ICE-like proteases), but the key downstream substrates whose cleavage directly activates the apoptotic phenotype, particularly DNA fragmentation, remain incompletely defined. Identifying this critical substrate is essential to understand the precise molecular switch from cellular commitment to death to the irreversible execution of apoptotic morphology. This knowledge would bridge the gap between caspase activation and the hallmark biochemical events of apoptosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, previously unidentified nuclear protein is the primary substrate of the executioner caspase-3 (CPP32/Yama/apopain), and its cleavage is the necessary and sufficient event to trigger the activation of the apoptotic DNA fragmentation machinery, including Caspase-Activated DNase (CAD).
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted biochemical purification approach using recombinant active caspase-3 to cleave nuclear extracts from human Jurkat T-cells. Cleavage products will be fractionated by HPLC and analyzed by Edman degradation and mass spectrometry (pre-2026 MALDI-TOF) to identify candidate substrates. Candidate proteins will be validated by in vitro cleavage assays, site-directed mutagenesis of the cleavage site (DXXD), and functional reconstitution in Apaf-1- or caspase-8-deficient cell lines to assess their sufficiency to induce DNA fragmentation upon ectopic expression of a pre-cleaved form.
proposals.contribution: This work will pinpoint the direct molecular link between caspase-3 activation and the dismantling of the nucleus, a central event in apoptosis. It will provide a definitive biochemical pathway for how the protease cascade translates into a specific apoptotic phenotype, potentially revealing a new regulatory node in cell death.
proposals.novelty: While CAD and its inhibitor ICAD are known caspase substrates, this proposal seeks the upstream caspase substrate whose cleavage is the pivotal event that licenses or directly activates the DNA fragmentation machinery, moving beyond the known regulator-inhibitor pair to find the initiating trigger.

proposals.title: Direct biochemical dissection of Bcl-2's inhibitory mechanism on cytosolic apoptosis signaling using a reconstituted in vitro system
proposals.motivation: The Bcl-2 protein family is a central regulator of the intrinsic apoptosis pathway, with anti-apoptotic members like Bcl-2 itself being critical in cancer cell survival and therapy resistance. However, the precise biochemical mechanism by which Bcl-2 inhibits the cytosolic activation of caspases, downstream of mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP), remains incompletely defined. A detailed mechanistic map is essential for developing next-generation BH3 mimetics and understanding apoptotic resistance in diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Bcl-2 directly sequesters and neutralizes a specific cytosolic factor(s) released from mitochondria, such as cytochrome c or Smac/DIABLO, thereby preventing the assembly or activity of the apoptosome and subsequent caspase-9 activation in the cytosol.
proposals.method: We will employ a fully defined, cell-free in vitro system reconstituted from purified human recombinant proteins: Apaf-1, procaspase-9, cytochrome c, dATP, and XIAP. We will titrate in purified, recombinant Bcl-2 protein and measure its effect on caspase-9 and caspase-3 activation kinetics using fluorogenic substrate assays. To pinpoint the target, we will perform sequential pre-incubation experiments, adding Bcl-2 to individual components (e.g., cytochrome c, Apaf-1, or the assembled apoptosome) before initiating the full reaction, followed by co-immunoprecipitation and native gel analysis to identify stable inhibitory complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, stepwise biochemical map of Bcl-2's point of inhibition within the cytosolic death machinery, distinguishing between models of apoptosome assembly blockade versus direct caspase inhibition. It will establish a foundational biochemical framework for evaluating the efficacy of novel BH3 mimetic compounds in disrupting specific protein-protein interactions critical for Bcl-2's anti-apoptotic function.
proposals.novelty: While Bcl-2's role at the mitochondria is well-studied, a purified, component-defined in vitro approach to dissect its direct action on the cytosolic execution phase is novel and bypasses the compensatory complexities of cellular systems, offering unambiguous mechanistic insight.

proposals.title: Decoupling Fas Expression from Intrinsic Inhibitors: A Quantitative Analysis of Apoptotic Susceptibility in Primary Human Cells
proposals.motivation: The Fas/FasL system is a cornerstone of apoptotic signaling in immune regulation and disease. However, clinical and experimental observations, including from our lab, show that high Fas surface expression does not universally predict apoptotic sensitivity, suggesting critical roles for intracellular regulators like c-FLIP. Resolving this discrepancy is essential for understanding immune homeostasis and designing therapies for autoimmune disorders and cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the susceptibility of a primary human cell to Fas-mediated apoptosis is not determined by Fas expression alone but is dictated by the stoichiometric ratio of Fas to key intracellular inhibitory proteins, particularly c-FLIP isoforms.
proposals.method: We will isolate primary T cells, hepatocytes, and epithelial cells from patient samples. Using quantitative flow cytometry, we will precisely measure surface Fas density. Concurrently, we will employ targeted mass spectrometry to quantify absolute protein levels of c-FLIP(L) and c-FLIP(S). Apoptotic sensitivity will be assayed by titrating recombinant human FasL and measuring caspase-8/3 activation and phosphatidylserine exposure. A multivariate regression model will be built to correlate the Fas:c-FLIP ratio with apoptotic thresholds across cell types.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, protein-level framework to predict apoptotic susceptibility, moving beyond a simple receptor-level view. It will clarify the mechanistic basis for cell-type-specific resistance to Fas signaling and identify potential biomarkers for pathological conditions characterized by dysregulated apoptosis.
proposals.novelty: While the roles of Fas and c-FLIP are known, a systematic, absolute quantitative analysis of their stoichiometry as the primary determinant of cell fate across diverse primary human cell types has not been performed, challenging the direct correlation assumption.

proposals.title: Divergent Apoptotic and Non-Apoptotic Signaling Outcomes of Fas Engagement Across Human Thymocyte Developmental Subsets
proposals.motivation: Fas (CD95) is a canonical death receptor critical for activation-induced cell death (AICD) in mature T cells and peripheral immune tolerance. However, its role in thymocyte development, particularly in negative selection and T cell repertoire shaping, remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 data suggest Fas expression varies across thymic subsets, but the functional consequence of this engagement is assumed to be uniform apoptosis, potentially overlooking critical subset-specific signaling that could influence T cell development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Fas engagement triggers distinct signaling outcomes—ranging from canonical apoptosis to non-apoptotic phenotypic modulation—in different thymocyte subsets (e.g., double-negative, double-positive, single-positive), dictated by subset-specific expression of intracellular regulators like c-FLIP, caspase-8 isoforms, and components of the necroptosis pathway.
proposals.method: We will isolate highly purified human thymocyte subsets by FACS from pediatric patient thymus samples (obtained during cardiac surgery). Using primary cells and in vitro-derived T-lineage cells from induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), we will perform multiplexed phospho-flow cytometry and western blotting to map signaling cascades (caspase-8 cleavage, RIPK1/3 phosphorylation, NF-κB activation) following stimulation with plate-bound anti-Fas agonist antibody (CH11) or recombinant FasL. Apoptotic (Annexin V/7-AAD) and necroptotic (MLKL phosphorylation, propidium iodide uptake with caspase inhibitor z-VAD-fmk) endpoints will be quantified. Genetic perturbation using CRISPR-Cas9 in iPSC-derived T-lineage cells will validate the role of specific regulators (e.g., c-FLIP, FADD) in determining subset-specific outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate a novel, context-dependent role for Fas in thymic development beyond simple apoptosis. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how a single death receptor can orchestrate multiple cellular fates, refining our understanding of central tolerance. The findings may explain discrepancies in murine versus human Fas biology and inform therapeutic strategies targeting Fas in autoimmune or lymphoproliferative diseases.
proposals.novelty: While Nagata's lab and others established Fas-mediated apoptosis, this proposal directly challenges the assumption of functional uniformity, seeking to identify non-apoptotic Fas signaling specifically in primary human thymocyte subsets, a context not systematically explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Direct Quantification of Fas Ligand-Mediated Epithelial Apoptosis in Human Colonic Tissue Using TUNEL Assay
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL)-induced apoptosis is a critical pathway in immune homeostasis and pathology, including inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). While FasL expression is known to be elevated in IBD, direct, quantitative correlation between FasL protein levels and actual epithelial cell death in human patient tissue biopsies remains technically challenging and often indirect. Establishing a robust method to link FasL expression to apoptotic outcome in situ is essential for understanding disease mechanisms and evaluating therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the level of FasL protein expression in colonic mucosal biopsies from patients with active IBD will show a strong, direct positive correlation with the frequency of apoptotic epithelial cells, as quantified by the TUNEL assay.
proposals.method: We will perform dual-label immunofluorescence and TUNEL staining on serial sections of paraffin-embedded colonic biopsies from patients with active ulcerative colitis and healthy controls. FasL will be detected using a validated anti-human FasL monoclonal antibody (e.g., NOK-1 clone) with a fluorescent secondary. Apoptotic cells will be identified using a commercial TUNEL assay kit (e.g., Roche In Situ Cell Death Detection Kit, TMR red). High-resolution confocal microscopy will be used for imaging. Quantification will involve counting TUNEL-positive epithelial nuclei and measuring FasL fluorescence intensity in adjacent epithelial and lamina propria regions using image analysis software (e.g., ImageJ/FIJI), followed by statistical correlation analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative spatial correlation between FasL expression and epithelial apoptosis in human IBD tissue. It will validate the functional significance of elevated FasL in disease pathogenesis. The established methodology will serve as a precise tool for future studies aiming to modulate the Fas/FasL pathway therapeutically.
proposals.novelty: While Nagata's lab has extensively characterized FasL-induced apoptosis mechanistically in vitro, applying the TUNEL assay directly to human patient biopsies to quantify this relationship in the complex tissue architecture of the colon is novel. This bridges the gap between molecular mechanism defined in model systems and human disease pathology.

proposals.title: Modeling immune-mediated epithelial turnover using patient-derived colonic organoids co-cultured with autologous FasL-expressing lymphocytes
proposals.motivation: Fas-FasL signaling is a critical pathway for cytotoxic lymphocyte-mediated apoptosis, essential for immune homeostasis and anti-tumor surveillance in the colon. However, existing models, such as 2D cell lines or mouse models, fail to recapitulate the human tissue architecture and autologous immune-epithelial interactions, limiting our understanding of physiological and dysregulated killing in contexts like inflammatory bowel disease or colorectal cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-culture of patient-derived colonic organoids with autologous, activated lymphocytes will establish a physiologically relevant ex vivo model of Fas-mediated epithelial cell death, revealing patient-specific variability in susceptibility to immune attack.
proposals.method: We will generate colonic organoids from endoscopic biopsies of patients. Autologous peripheral blood lymphocytes (PBLs) will be activated with anti-CD3/CD28 beads and IL-2 to induce FasL expression. Organoids and PBLs will be co-cultured in Matrigel-embedded conditions. Apoptosis in organoids will be quantified by live-cell imaging using caspase-3/7 activity reporters and by flow cytometry of dissociated organoid cells for Annexin V and propidium iodide staining. Specificity for the Fas pathway will be confirmed using the antagonistic anti-Fas antibody ZB4 or soluble Fas-Fc decoy protein.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, patient-specific experimental platform to study immune-epithelial interactions in a human tissue context. It will define quantitative parameters of Fas-mediated killing in normal and diseased colonic epithelium and could identify intrinsic epithelial factors that confer resistance or sensitivity to lymphocyte attack.
proposals.novelty: While organoids are established for epithelial biology, their systematic co-culture with autologous immune cells to model a specific death receptor pathway is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk cytokine assays to directly visualize and quantify cytotoxic interactions within a 3D tissue structure.

proposals.title: Soluble Fas Receptor Shedding by Synovial Cells as a Counter-Regulatory Mechanism to sFasL in Rheumatoid Arthritis
proposals.motivation: In rheumatoid arthritis (RA), synovial hyperplasia and inflammation are linked to dysregulated apoptosis. Soluble Fas ligand (sFasL) is elevated in RA synovial fluid and can induce apoptosis, yet synovial cells persist. A known counter-mechanism is the shedding of soluble Fas (sFas), which acts as a decoy receptor, but whether synovial cells actively upregulate this shedding specifically in response to sFasL remains unclear. Elucidating this dynamic could explain local resistance to Fas-mediated apoptosis in RA.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synovial fibroblasts and macrophages, upon exposure to pathophysiological levels of sFasL, actively upregulate the metalloprotease-mediated shedding of membrane Fas receptor, generating sFas as a feedback mechanism to limit further apoptotic signaling.
proposals.method: Primary human synovial fibroblasts and macrophages from RA patients will be cultured and stimulated with recombinant human sFasL. sFas in supernatants will be quantified by specific ELISA. Membrane Fas expression will be analyzed by flow cytometry. The role of ADAM10 and ADAM17 will be tested using specific pharmacological inhibitors (GI254023X, TAPI-2) and siRNA knockdown. Apoptosis will be measured by Annexin V/PI staining and caspase-3/7 activity assays to correlate sFas shedding with functional protection.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly demonstrate a novel, active feedback loop where sFasL triggers its own inhibition via receptor shedding in the RA joint. It will identify the key sheddase involved, providing a potential mechanistic explanation for the paradoxical coexistence of high sFasL and synovial cell survival. This could reveal new therapeutic targets to re-sensitize pathogenic cells to apoptosis.
proposals.novelty: While sFas is known to be present in RA, its dynamic, ligand-induced generation by resident synovial cells as an immediate protective response has not been established. This moves beyond correlative observation to a causative, mechanistic investigation of the Fas system's autoregulation in a disease-relevant context.

proposals.title: Genetic and Contextual Dissection of Fas-Independent Apoptosis in RP105-Deficient B Cell Blasts
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Nagata's group established that B cell blasts from RP105-deficient mice undergo apoptosis upon anti-IgM stimulation via a novel, Apaf-1/Caspase-8-independent pathway, suggesting Fas independence. However, Fas/FasL is a canonical death receptor pathway crucial in lymphocyte homeostasis and autoimmunity. It remains unresolved whether Fas can contribute to this apoptotic response under specific genetic backgrounds or co-stimulatory conditions, a critical question for understanding apoptotic redundancy and B cell fate decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while RP105-mediated apoptosis is primarily Fas-independent, the Fas pathway can be engaged to contribute to cell death in anti-IgM-stimulated B cell blasts under specific genetic or contextual perturbations, such as in Fas-sensitive mouse strains or with concurrent TLR or cytokine signaling.
proposals.method: We will generate RP105-deficient B cell blasts from C57BL/6 (standard), MRL/lpr (Fas-mutant), and BALB/c (reported Fas-sensitive strain) mice. Apoptosis will be quantified by Annexin V/PI flow cytometry and caspase-3/7 activity assays following anti-IgM stimulation, with or without blocking anti-FasL antibody (MFL3) or recombinant Fas-Fc protein. We will further modulate context using co-stimulation with TLR ligands (e.g., LPS, CpG) or cytokines (IL-4, IFN-γ). Molecular pathway engagement will be assessed by immunoblotting for caspase-8 cleavage, FLIP expression, and IAP proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the boundaries of Fas independence in this novel apoptotic pathway, clarifying whether Fas can serve as a fail-safe or synergistic mechanism. It will provide a more nuanced model of B cell apoptosis regulation, with implications for understanding B cell lymphomas and autoimmune disorders where RP105 and Fas signaling are implicated.
proposals.novelty: While the Fas-independent nature of the pathway is established, systematically testing for conditional Fas involvement across genetic backgrounds and inflammatory contexts is novel. It moves beyond a binary classification to explore apoptotic pathway plasticity and redundancy in a defined cellular system.

proposals.title: Biased Ligand Engagement of FcγRIIB Reveals Context-Dependent Regulation of RP105-Mediated Apoptosis
proposals.motivation: RP105 (CD180) is a TLR-like receptor that can induce apoptosis in B cells and macrophages, a process reported to be strongly augmented by co-ligation with the inhibitory receptor FcγRIIB. However, the field lacks a mechanistic understanding of how this co-ligation universally enhances a death signal. This gap limits our ability to therapeutically modulate these pathways in autoimmune or lymphoproliferative diseases. The current model treats FcγRIIB engagement as a binary switch, which is inconsistent with the nuanced, ligand-dependent signaling seen in other receptor systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FcγRIIB does not have a uniform pro-apoptotic function but acts as a context-dependent regulator of RP105 signaling, where the specific molecular mode of co-engagement (determined by ligand isotype, affinity, and geometry) dictates the functional outcome, ranging from strong augmentation to inhibition of apoptosis.
proposals.method: We will engineer a panel of bispecific antibodies and Fc-fusion proteins to systematically vary the ligand properties for the RP105/FcγRIIB complex, including IgG subclass (IgG1, IgG2a), F(ab') orientation, and ligand valency/density. Using human B cell lines and primary human B cells from patient samples, we will measure apoptosis (via Annexin V/PI and caspase-3/7 activation), downstream signaling (phosphorylation of SYK, PI3K, and MAPK pathways), and gene expression. Statistical analysis will correlate specific engagement parameters with signaling clusters and apoptotic outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond a simplistic 'on/off' model of FcγRIIB function in apoptosis, establishing a ligand-defined spectrum of activity. It will provide a precise molecular toolkit for dissecting co-receptor signaling and identify specific engagement modes that could be targeted to either enhance or suppress RP105-mediated cell death for therapeutic benefit.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the neuropharmacological concept of 'biased agonism' or functional selectivity—well-established for GPCRs but largely unexplored in immune receptor pairs—to explain the contradictory regulatory potentials of FcγRIIB, challenging its assumed uniform role in RP105-mediated apoptosis.

proposals.title: Tfh-CD40 signaling networks that antagonize BCR-induced apoptosis in germinal center B cells
proposals.motivation: Germinal center (GC) B cells undergo iterative cycles of somatic hypermutation and selection based on BCR affinity. High-affinity BCR engagement promotes survival, but paradoxically, BCR cross-linking in vitro triggers apoptosis in activated B cells. In vivo, T follicular helper (Tfh) cell-derived CD40L is essential for GC persistence, yet the precise molecular circuitry by which physiological CD40 signaling overrides BCR-induced apoptotic death remains a critical gap. Defining this network is fundamental to understanding humoral immunity and lymphomagenesis, where dysregulated CD40 signaling is implicated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Tfh-delivered CD40 signaling activates a specific, temporally regulated phospho-signaling network that transcriptionally and post-translationally reprograms B cells to express key anti-apoptotic factors, thereby establishing a state of resistance to BCR-mediated apoptosis.
proposals.method: We will establish primary mouse and human B/Tfh co-culture systems using antigen-specific B cells and Tfh cells. Following physiological CD40 engagement, B cells will be challenged with anti-BCR. Temporal phosphoproteomic profiling (using TiO2 enrichment and LC-MS/MS) will map signaling dynamics. In parallel, we will perform CRISPR/Cas9-based functional genomics screens in B cells co-cultured with Tfh cells to identify genes essential for CD40-conferred apoptosis resistance. Key hits will be validated by generating conditional knockout mice (e.g., using CD23-Cre) to assess GC B cell competitive fitness and apoptosis sensitivity in vivo.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of the phospho-signaling network activated by physiological Tfh-CD40 engagement. It will identify novel molecular nodes essential for protecting GC B cells from apoptosis, offering mechanistic insight into GC selection. The findings may reveal new therapeutic targets for modulating antibody responses or treating B cell lymphomas driven by apoptotic evasion.
proposals.novelty: While CD40's pro-survival role is known, this study uniquely focuses on its specific function in antagonizing the BCR's intrinsic apoptotic signal using physiological Tfh help, moving beyond simplified anti-CD40 antibody stimulation. The integration of temporal phosphoproteomics with context-specific functional genomics in a primary co-culture system represents a novel, systems-level approach to dissect this cell-contact-dependent survival pathway.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal mapping of oncogenic transition by inducible blockade of Fas and caspase-8 signaling in epithelial tissues
proposals.motivation: The loss of apoptosis is a hallmark of cancer, but the precise sequence of events linking blocked cell death to dysregulated, pre-oncogenic proliferation remains unclear. While compensatory proliferation following apoptosis is a known tissue repair mechanism, the specific signaling nodes and temporal dynamics that cause this process to become pathological are not defined. This gap limits our ability to intervene in early carcinogenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute, cell-type-specific blockade of the Fas/FasL or caspase-8-mediated extrinsic apoptosis pathway will initiate a defined temporal program of compensatory proliferation, and that sustained blockade past a critical time window will lead to oncogenic hyperplasia driven by specific downstream inflammatory and survival signals.
proposals.method: We will generate transgenic mouse models expressing doxycycline-inducible, dominant-negative mutants of Fas (dnFas) or caspase-8 (dnCasp8) specifically in keratinocytes or intestinal epithelia. Using these models, we will acutely inhibit apoptosis in vivo and perform longitudinal analysis. We will combine timed tissue sampling for histology and BrdU/EdU labeling with transcriptomic (RNA-seq) and phospho-proteomic profiling at defined intervals post-induction to map the transition from normal homeostasis to hyperplasia.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal, high-resolution timeline linking the inhibition of specific apoptotic pathways to the onset of pathological hyperplasia. It will identify the key molecular switches and signaling cascades (e.g., NF-κB, JNK, or STAT3 activation) that drive the shift from compensatory to dysregulated proliferation, providing potential early intervention targets.
proposals.novelty: Unlike static knockout models, our inducible dominant-negative system allows precise temporal control to dissect the sequence of events, directly testing the causal transition from blocked apoptosis to oncogenic progression in specific cell types in vivo.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Non-Canonical Apoptotic Pathway: A Dominant-Negative Mutant Approach to Define the Essential Signaling Architecture
proposals.motivation: The Nagata lab's discovery of Apaf-1- and Caspase-8-independent apoptosis reveals a significant gap in our understanding of cell death pathways. Given the clinical relevance of FasL/Fas signaling in immune regulation and disease, it is critical to define the precise molecular players and their hierarchical relationships in this alternative pathway. Understanding this pathway's necessity could reveal new therapeutic targets for conditions where canonical apoptosis is dysregulated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Apaf-1/Caspase-8-independent apoptotic pathway initiated by FasL requires a specific, non-redundant set of signaling proteins downstream of the Fas receptor. By selectively blocking candidate proteins with dominant-negative mutants, we can delineate the essential nodes of this pathway.
proposals.method: We will engineer a panel of dominant-negative (DN) mutants targeting key signaling proteins implicated in apoptosis (e.g., FADD, RIPK1, Caspase-10, other death domain-containing proteins) based on pre-2026 structural knowledge. These DN constructs, along with a control DN-FADD mutant known to block the canonical pathway, will be stably expressed in human cell lines sensitive to FasL-induced, Apaf-1-independent apoptosis. Apoptotic fate will be quantified by Annexin V/PI flow cytometry, caspase-3/7 activity assays, and the lab's established DNA fragmentation assays (measuring caspase-activated DNase activity). Pathway necessity will be determined by the ability of each DN mutant to specifically rescue cell death.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive functional map of the essential signaling components in the non-canonical apoptotic pathway, moving beyond correlative observations. It will clarify whether this pathway represents a backup mechanism or a primary death signaling route in specific cellular contexts. The findings could identify novel molecular targets for modulating apoptosis in therapeutic settings.
proposals.novelty: While DN mutants are a classic tool, their systematic application to dissect this newly identified, ill-defined apoptotic pathway is novel. This approach directly tests the functional requirement of specific proteins in a pathway where genetic redundancy or compensatory mechanisms have obscured essential roles.

proposals.title: Integration of BCR Signal Kinetics and N-Terminomics Reveals Activation History-Dependent Caspase Substrate Cleavage in Fas-Mediated Apoptosis of Human B Cells
proposals.motivation: B cell fate decisions, including apoptosis, are critically shaped by B cell receptor (BCR) signaling dynamics. While Fas ligand (FasL)-induced apoptosis is a key regulatory mechanism, it remains unclear how prior BCR activation history, encoded in specific calcium flux patterns, influences the downstream proteolytic execution phase. Defining this link is essential to understand immune tolerance and lymphomagenesis, where dysregulated BCR signaling and apoptotic resistance converge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct kinetic profiles of early BCR signaling (e.g., amplitude, duration) in different human B cell subsets are functionally encoded into divergent caspase-mediated proteolytic programs during Fas-induced apoptosis, leading to the cleavage of a unique repertoire of substrates.
proposals.method: We will isolate human B cell subsets (e.g., naive, RP105+ marginal zone-like, CD40L/IL-4-stimulated blasts) and perform single-cell calcium imaging using Fura-2 AM to capture BCR-induced Ca2+ flux kinetics. Subsequently, the same pre-profiled cells will be treated with recombinant FasL to induce apoptosis. Cells will be sorted based on their prior calcium signature, and apoptotic proteolysis will be analyzed by N-terminomics (TAILS), a systematic proteomics method to identify neo-N-terminal peptides and thus catalog caspase cleavage events. Correlation analysis will link specific calcium flux parameters to substrate cleavage patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, functional link between early signal dynamics and the apoptotic execution phase, generating the first comprehensive map of caspase substrates whose cleavage is contingent on B cell activation history. It will identify novel proteolytic events that may dictate cell fate decisions, offering potential mechanistic insights into B cell pathologies and therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of live-cell kinetic signaling analysis with systematic, unbiased proteolytic profiling in the same cell population moves beyond static correlations to establish causal links between signal history and apoptotic execution, a non-obvious approach given existing work which typically studies these phases in isolation.

proposals.title: Microarray Profiling of RP105- and CD40-Activated B Cell Blasts Reveals a Gene Expression Signature Governing Susceptibility to FasL-Mediated Apoptosis
proposals.motivation: B cell activation via CD40 or the TLR-family member RP105 leads to proliferation but confers differential susceptibility to Fas ligand (FasL)-induced apoptosis, a key regulatory mechanism in immune homeostasis. The molecular basis for this differential sensitivity remains poorly defined, limiting our understanding of B cell fate decisions in immunity and autoimmunity. Pre-2026, cDNA microarrays are a powerful, established tool for unbiased gene expression analysis, ideal for dissecting this complex cellular phenotype.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RP105- and CD40-activated human B cell blasts exhibit distinct, definable gene expression profiles, and that the differential expression of a specific set of pro-apoptotic, anti-apoptotic, or signal-modulating genes directly dictates their susceptibility to Fas-mediated apoptosis.
proposals.method: Human primary B cells will be activated with anti-CD40 or anti-RP105 antibodies to generate blasts. Apoptosis susceptibility will be confirmed by treatment with recombinant FasL. Total RNA from susceptible (RP105) and resistant (CD40) blasts will be extracted, reverse-transcribed, and labeled with Cy3/Cy5 for competitive hybridization on comprehensive human cDNA microarray platforms (e.g., Agilent). Differentially expressed genes will be identified using significance analysis of microarrays (SAM) with a false discovery rate (FDR) <5%. Key candidates (e.g., novel IAPs, FLIP isoforms, or Bcl-2 family members) will be validated by quantitative RT-PCR and their functional role tested via siRNA knockdown or overexpression in primary blasts followed by apoptosis assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the first comprehensive gene expression signature underlying Fas sensitivity in activated human B cells, moving beyond single-molecule studies. It will identify novel genetic regulators of apoptosis that may serve as therapeutic targets for modulating aberrant B cell survival in lymphomas or autoimmune diseases. The data will provide a resource linking specific B cell activation pathways to downstream fate-determining programs.
proposals.novelty: While Nagata's lab has extensively characterized the Fas/FasL system and apoptotic machinery, applying genome-wide expression profiling to compare these two specific B cell activation pathways in the context of apoptosis regulation is novel. This approach moves from a known phenotype to an unbiased discovery of its multilayered genetic basis, potentially revealing non-canonical regulators beyond the core apoptosis pathway.

proposals.title: Reprogramming Apoptosis Resistance in B Cell Lymphomas via High-Dimensional Mapping and Sequential Receptor Engagement
proposals.motivation: B cell lymphomas frequently develop resistance to B cell receptor (BCR)-targeted therapies, a major clinical challenge. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab on FasL-induced apoptosis and caspase-independent death pathways, highlights that apoptosis is regulated by complex signaling networks. However, a systematic, dynamic understanding of how co-stimulatory receptors like CD40 and RP105 rewire these networks to sustain survival in resistant primary tumors is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that apoptosis resistance in primary B cell lymphomas is maintained by specific, redundant signaling architectures downstream of BCR, CD40, and RP105, and that these can be overcome by rationally sequenced receptor engagements designed to collapse compensatory pro-survival pathways.
proposals.method: We will use primary B cell lymphomas clinically resistant to BCR-induced apoptosis. High-throughput phospho-flow cytometry (using pre-2026 panels and CyTOF/mass cytometry) will quantify dynamic phosphorylation states of NF-κB, MAPK, and PI3K/AKT pathways upon individual and combinatorial stimulation of BCR, RP105, and CD40. Signaling profiles will be correlated with functional apoptosis readouts. Dimensionality reduction (t-SNE) and clustering algorithms will identify resistance-associated signaling states, guiding the design of sequential receptor engagement protocols to test for restored apoptosis.
proposals.contribution: This will generate a first-of-its-kind signaling cartography of apoptosis resistance in primary human B cell lymphomas, linking specific network states to functional outcomes. It will move the field from static biomarker discovery to a dynamic, engineering-based framework for overcoming treatment resistance by identifying targetable signaling bottlenecks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous studies focusing on single pathways or static susceptibility, this approach dynamically maps the high-dimensional signaling network and actively tests combinatorial, sequential receptor perturbations to reprogram, rather than simply probe, the apoptotic threshold.

proposals.title: Stromal Survival Signals Reversibly Inhibit Executioner Caspases and MOMP in B Cells Following Pro-Apoptotic RP105/BCR Engagement
proposals.motivation: The B cell fate decision between survival and apoptosis is critical for immune homeostasis and is dictated by the integration of signals from the B cell receptor (BCR), co-receptors like RP105, and the stromal microenvironment. While survival signals are known to block apoptosis initiation, it remains unknown if they can actively reverse the apoptotic cascade after key execution-phase events, such as mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP) or caspase-3/7 activation, have been triggered. Determining the plasticity of these late-stage events is fundamental to understanding cell fate commitment and has implications for lymphoid malignancies where microenvironmental protection is a therapeutic challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific cytokine signals from the stromal microenvironment can actively reverse the execution phase of apoptosis, including caspase activation and MOMP, in human B cells following pro-apoptotic RP105/BCR engagement, and that this reversibility is governed by a defined molecular checkpoint prior to the activation of caspase-activated DNase (CAD).
proposals.method: We will use primary human B cells and a co-culture system with stromal cells expressing defined ligands (e.g., BAFF, IL-4). Apoptosis will be triggered via anti-RP105/BCR crosslinking. Using live-cell imaging with FRET-based caspase-3/7 biosensors and mitochondrial membrane potential dyes (e.g., TMRE), we will precisely time the addition of stromal-derived conditioned media or recombinant cytokines relative to MOMP and caspase activation. Reversibility will be quantified by measuring the restoration of mitochondrial integrity, deactivation of caspases (using activity probes and immunoblotting for cleaved substrates), and ultimate cell viability. The involvement of specific inhibitors like XIAP will be tested using siRNA knockdown.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a precise temporal map of apoptotic reversibility in primary human B cells, identifying the 'point of no return' within the execution phase. It will define which microenvironmental signals are capable of reversing late apoptotic events, challenging the classical view of MOMP as an irreversible commitment step. The findings will provide a new mechanistic framework for how stromal niches protect B cells, with direct relevance to understanding treatment resistance in B-cell lymphomas.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Nagata's prior work defined key linear components of apoptosis (e.g., FasL-induced death, CAD regulation), this proposal directly tests the non-linear plasticity and active reversibility of the execution phase, a concept not explored in the context of B cell microenvironmental signaling. It moves beyond blockade to ask if cell death can be 'rewound' by extracellular cues.

proposals.title: Stromal Survival Signals Override RP105/BCR-Mediated Apoptosis in B-Cell Malignancies
proposals.motivation: RP105, a TLR-like molecule, transmits a pro-apoptotic signal upon BCR engagement in certain B-cell lymphomas, representing a potential tumor-suppressive pathway. However, tumor cells persist in vivo, suggesting the microenvironment provides protective signals. Understanding how stromal survival signals dominate over intrinsic apoptotic triggers is crucial for developing therapies that can overcome microenvironment-mediated drug resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific cytokines (e.g., BAFF, IL-4) or direct stromal cell contact activate anti-apoptotic pathways (e.g., NF-κB, PI3K/Akt) that suppress the caspase-dependent and -independent apoptotic machinery initiated by RP105/BCR co-engagement.
proposals.method: We will use primary human B-cell malignancy samples and co-culture them with stromal cell lines (e.g., HS-5). Apoptosis induced by anti-RP105/BCR crosslinking will be quantified by Annexin V/PI staining and caspase-3/7 activity assays. The role of specific cytokines will be tested using neutralizing antibodies and recombinant proteins. Key apoptotic and survival pathways will be analyzed by immunoblotting for phospho-proteins (Akt, IκBα, STATs) and by assessing mitochondrial membrane potential. The functional role of candidate pathways will be confirmed using specific pharmacological inhibitors (e.g., Akt inhibitor MK-2206).
proposals.contribution: This work will identify the dominant survival pathways that protect malignant B-cells from RP105/BCR-induced apoptosis, defining a key mechanism of microenvironmental resistance. It will provide a rationale for combining BCR-targeting strategies with inhibitors of specific stromal survival signals to improve therapeutic outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While Nagata's lab has extensively mapped core apoptotic machinery, applying this knowledge to the context of RP105 signaling and its override by the microenvironment is unexplored. This bridges the study of intrinsic death pathways with extrinsic survival signaling in a clinically relevant model.

proposals.title: Conditional Regulation of Fas-Mediated Apoptosis by the Tyrosine Phosphatase PTP-BL
proposals.motivation: Fas signaling is a critical pathway for immune homeostasis and cytotoxicity, but its regulation in physiological, non-saturating conditions remains incompletely understood. The protein tyrosine phosphatase PTP-BL has been implicated in apoptosis, yet its specific role in modulating the Fas death receptor under varied signaling strengths is unknown. Defining context-dependent regulators is essential for understanding apoptotic sensitivity in human diseases, including autoimmunity and cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PTP-BL acts as a conditional negative regulator of Fas signaling, exerting its inhibitory effect primarily under sub-saturating apoptotic stimuli or in specific cellular differentiation states, thereby setting a threshold for apoptosis.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR-Cas9 to generate PTP-BL knockout and overexpression lines in human Jurkat T-cells and primary patient-derived T-cell blasts. Apoptosis will be induced with titrated doses of recombinant human FasL or anti-Fas agonistic antibody (CH11). Signaling dynamics will be assessed by immunoblotting for caspase-8 cleavage, FLIP expression, and receptor tyrosine phosphorylation. Cell state dependency will be tested by comparing activated versus resting primary T-cells and by modulating cellular redox state with N-acetylcysteine.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish PTP-BL as a context-dependent rheostat for Fas signaling, moving beyond binary regulator classifications. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how sub-maximal death receptor engagement is integrated at the molecular level. The findings could identify new targets for modulating pathological T-cell survival in human patients.
proposals.novelty: While PTP-BL's interaction with Fas is known, its function has not been systematically tested under physiologically relevant, non-saturating stimulus conditions. This proposal uniquely focuses on the conditional nature of this regulation, linking phosphatase activity to apoptotic threshold setting in specific cell states.

proposals.title: A conserved C-terminal motif dictates species-specific regulation of Fas receptor: Functional analysis of a humanized mouse Fas chimera
proposals.motivation: The Fas death receptor is a critical mediator of apoptosis in immune regulation and disease. While the core apoptotic signaling domain (death domain) is well-characterized, the regulatory role of the C-terminal region, particularly the conserved 'tS-L-V' motif in humans, remains poorly understood. Defining the function of this motif is essential for understanding species-specific differences in Fas regulation and could reveal novel post-translational control mechanisms for apoptosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the human-specific C-terminal tS-L-V motif in Fas confers unique regulatory properties, such as differential ubiquitination or protein-protein interactions, that are not present in the mouse receptor, and that engineering this motif into mouse Fas will alter its stability, internalization, or signaling output.
proposals.method: We will use site-directed mutagenesis and gene synthesis to create a chimeric mouse Fas receptor where its C-terminal amino acids are replaced with the human tS-L-V motif. This construct will be stably expressed in Fas-deficient mouse T-cell lines (e.g., L1210). Apoptotic sensitivity will be tested by treating cells with recombinant mouse FasL and anti-Fas antibodies (Jo2). We will assess receptor turnover via cycloheximide chase and metabolic labeling, and analyze ubiquitination status via immunoprecipitation and anti-ubiquitin immunoblotting. Interactions will be probed by co-immunoprecipitation with known human Fas-binding proteins like FAF1.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the functional significance of a conserved but uncharacterized human Fas motif, moving beyond the death domain to define C-terminal regulation. It will clarify if observed species differences in Fas signaling are rooted in this sequence, providing a mechanistic basis for interpreting mouse model data in the context of human biology. The findings may identify a new regulatory node for therapeutic modulation of Fas-mediated cell death.
proposals.novelty: While chimeric receptors are common, this study uniquely focuses on a short, non-signaling C-terminal motif of unknown function, distinct from the well-studied death domain. It directly tests a specific molecular determinant of species-specific regulation that has been noted in sequence alignments but never functionally interrogated.

proposals.title: Dissecting TCR Proximal Signaling for Fas Ligand Expression: A Specific Role for CD3ζ ITAMs
proposals.motivation: T cell receptor (TCR) engagement induces Fas ligand (FasL) expression, a critical mediator of activation-induced cell death (AICD) and immune regulation. While the CD3ζ chain's ITAM motifs are known to be important, it remains unclear whether they are uniquely required or if other proximal TCR signals (e.g., from CD3ε, δ, γ chains or co-receptors like CD28) can compensate to induce FasL when ζ-chain signaling is specifically disabled. Resolving this hierarchy is essential for understanding the precise molecular checkpoints controlling T cell apoptosis and peripheral tolerance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ITAM motifs of the CD3ζ chain are the sole non-redundant initiators of TCR-induced FasL expression, and that signals from other TCR-proximal components cannot induce FasL when ζ-chain ITAM function is abrogated.
proposals.method: We will use primary human T cells and Jurkat T cell lines engineered with CRISPR-Cas9 to express a mutant CD3ζ chain where critical tyrosine residues in all ITAMs are mutated to phenylalanine (Y-to-F). These cells will be stimulated via the TCR using anti-CD3 antibodies or superantigens, and via alternative pathways using PMA/ionomycin or specific co-stimulatory receptors (e.g., anti-CD28). FasL expression will be quantified by flow cytometry and qRT-PCR. We will further dissect contributions by reconstituting mutant cells with chimeric receptors swapping signaling domains from CD3ε, CD28, or LAT onto the cytoplasmic tail of CD3ζ to test for rescue of FasL induction.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the TCR-proximal signaling requirements for FasL induction, clarifying whether CD3ζ ITAMs hold a privileged position. It will advance our understanding of AICD specificity and could identify potential molecular targets for modulating FasL-dependent pathologies, such as autoimmune diseases or graft-versus-host disease, without globally impairing T cell activation.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies established the importance of CD3ζ for FasL induction but did not systematically test if its ITAM motifs are uniquely essential or if other proximal signals are sufficient when ζ is disabled, a key distinction for understanding signal specificity in AICD.

proposals.title: Elucidating the TCR/CD3ζ-ARH1 Motif-Dependent Signaling Cascade for Transcriptional Activation of the Fas Ligand Gene
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL) expression on activated T cells is critical for activation-induced cell death (AICD) and immune homeostasis. While the CD3-zeta chain's ARH-1 (antigen recognition homology-1) motifs are known to be essential for FasL induction upon TCR engagement, the specific signaling molecules bridging these motifs to the FasL promoter remain undefined. Mapping this pathway is crucial for understanding immune regulation and pathologies involving dysregulated T cell death.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, non-canonical signaling module, involving the sequential recruitment and activation of defined adaptor proteins and kinases downstream of CD3-zeta ARH-1 motifs, is responsible for the transcriptional activation of the FasL gene.
proposals.method: We will employ a systematic, loss-of-function approach in human Jurkat T cells and primary human T cells. Using CRISPR-Cas9, we will generate knockout lines for candidate signaling molecules (e.g., ZAP-70 isoforms, SLP-76, Vav, specific PKC isoforms, MAPKs). FasL mRNA and protein expression will be quantified by RT-qPCR and flow cytometry after anti-CD3 stimulation. Signaling complex composition will be analyzed by co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectrometry using CD3-zeta ARH-1 motif-specific bait constructs. Key interactions will be validated by reconstitution experiments with wild-type and mutant proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the core signaling pathway linking TCR engagement to FasL gene expression, filling a major gap in our understanding of AICD. It will identify potential therapeutic targets for modulating FasL-dependent apoptosis in autoimmune diseases, graft rejection, or cancer immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While ARH-1 motifs are known to be necessary, this study will be the first to comprehensively map the specific, obligate signaling components they recruit for FasL induction, moving beyond the general requirement of these motifs to a mechanistic pathway.

proposals.title: Dissecting the TCR/CD3 Signaling Requirements for Fas Ligand- versus Perforin/Granzyme-Mediated Cytotoxicity in Human Cytotoxic T Lymphocytes
proposals.motivation: Cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) eliminate target cells primarily via the Fas/FasL pathway and the perforin/granzyme pathway. While both are triggered by T cell receptor (TCR) engagement, it remains unclear if the upstream signaling cascades that license these effector mechanisms are identical or diverge. Understanding this is crucial for deciphering immune regulation and developing therapies for autoimmune diseases and cancer where these pathways are dysregulated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the TCR/CD3 signaling requirements for FasL upregulation are distinct from those for perforin/granzyme exocytosis, with differential dependence on specific kinase cascades and calcium flux.
proposals.method: We will use primary human CTLs from healthy donors and patients with defined signaling deficiencies. Using specific pharmacological inhibitors (e.g., for ZAP-70, Lck, calcineurin) and genetic approaches (CRISPR-Cas9 knockout in primary T cells), we will dissect signaling requirements. Cytotoxicity will be measured in real-time against Fas-expressing and Fas-deficient target cells. FasL surface expression will be quantified by flow cytometry, and perforin/granzyme release will be assessed by granzyme B ELISpot and CD107a degranulation assays upon stimulation with anti-CD3/CD28 beads or antigen-presenting cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a potential signaling 'checkpoint' that differentially regulates the two major cytotoxic pathways. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding how CTLs choose between apoptotic mechanisms, with implications for manipulating immune responses in pathology.
proposals.novelty: While the pathways themselves are well-studied, a direct, systematic comparison of their proximal TCR signaling dependencies in primary human CTLs has not been performed. This approach moves beyond correlative studies to establish causal signaling requirements.

proposals.title: Identification of Intrinsic and Extrinsic Inhibitory Pathways for TCR-Induced Fas Ligand Expression in Human T Cells
proposals.motivation: TCR-induced Fas ligand (FasL) expression is critical for activation-induced cell death (AICD) and immune homeostasis, but its dysregulation causes immunopathology. While signals promoting FasL expression are known, the mechanisms that actively suppress it to prevent excessive T cell cytotoxicity and tissue damage remain poorly defined. Identifying these inhibitory pathways is essential for understanding immune tolerance and developing therapies for autoimmune and lymphoproliferative diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific inhibitory receptors (e.g., PD-1, CTLA-4) and cytokine signals (e.g., IL-10, TGF-β) actively suppress TCR-induced FasL transcription in human T cells through defined intracellular signaling nodes, such as SHP-1/2 phosphatases and STAT3.
proposals.method: We will use primary human T cells from healthy donors and patients with autoimmune disorders. FasL expression will be quantified by flow cytometry and qRT-PCR after TCR stimulation with anti-CD3/CD28 beads in the presence of candidate inhibitors. A CRISPR-Cas9 screen in Jurkat T cells, using a guide RNA library targeting known inhibitory receptors and signaling molecules, will identify genes whose knockout enhances TCR-induced FasL reporter activity. Hits will be validated by reconstitution experiments and analysis of signaling pathways (phospho-flow, immunoblot).
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel regulatory network for FasL expression, identifying key 'brakes' on T cell cytotoxicity. It will provide mechanistic insight into how immune responses are constrained and may reveal new therapeutic targets for modulating FasL-dependent immunopathology in autoimmunity and cancer.
proposals.novelty: While Nagata's prior work defined the Fas/FasL apoptosis pathway, this proposal inverts the question to focus on active suppression of FasL, a less-studied aspect of immune regulation. Combining patient-derived cells with a targeted CRISPR screen specifically for FasL inhibitors is a novel, hypothesis-driven approach to this inverse problem.

proposals.title: Functional compensation for Fas deficiency by pro-survival Bcl-2 family proteins in vivo
proposals.motivation: The Fas/FasL system is a critical pathway for apoptosis in immune regulation and homeostasis. Fas-deficient mice (lpr) develop lymphoproliferative and autoimmune disorders, but the apoptotic machinery remains partially functional, suggesting compensatory mechanisms. Given the established role of Bcl-2 family proteins in regulating the mitochondrial apoptotic pathway, it is crucial to determine if they can functionally substitute for defects in the death receptor pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that overexpression of pro-survival Bcl-2 family members, specifically Bcl-2 or Bcl-xL, will suppress the lymphoproliferative and autoimmune phenotypes in Fas-deficient mice by inhibiting the mitochondrial apoptosis pathway, thereby compensating for the defective extrinsic death receptor signal.
proposals.method: We will generate double-transgenic mice by crossing Fas-deficient C57BL/6-lpr/lpr mice with transgenic mice expressing human Bcl-2 or Bcl-xL under the control of the lck promoter (T-cell specific) or the Eμ promoter (B-cell specific). Lymphoid organ size, cellularity, and lymphocyte subsets (CD4+, CD8+, B220+) will be analyzed by flow cytometry. Apoptosis sensitivity will be tested ex vivo using anti-CD3/anti-Fas stimulation and intrinsic pathway inducers (e.g., staurosporine). Autoimmune markers (serum immunoglobulins, anti-dsDNA antibodies) and histopathology of kidneys will be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the in vivo capacity of Bcl-2 and Bcl-xL to compensate for a defective extrinsic apoptosis pathway, clarifying the crosstalk between death receptor and mitochondrial pathways in a physiological disease model. It will identify which pro-survival protein is most effective in suppressing lpr-associated pathology, providing mechanistic insight for potential therapeutic strategies targeting Bcl-2 family proteins in apoptosis-related disorders.
proposals.novelty: While Bcl-2 overexpression is known to inhibit apoptosis, its specific ability to rescue the complex autoimmune phenotype of Fas deficiency in vivo has not been systematically tested and compared directly with Bcl-xL, offering a direct assessment of functional redundancy between major apoptotic regulatory hubs.
