proposals.title: Dual Inhibition of Extrinsic Apoptosis and Promotion of Intrinsic Survival Uncouples Hematopoietic Stem Cell Maintenance from Differentiation
proposals.motivation: Hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) homeostasis is tightly regulated by a balance of pro- and anti-apoptotic signals. Pre-2026 work has shown that Fas-mediated extrinsic apoptosis and Mcl-1-dependent intrinsic survival are critical for hematopoietic regulation, but their combined, cell-autonomous manipulation in HSCs remains unexplored. Understanding this interplay is crucial for developing strategies to expand HSCs for transplantation and to treat hematological malignancies where these pathways are often dysregulated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combined genetic disruption of the Fas death receptor and enforced overexpression of the anti-apoptotic protein Mcl-1 will synergistically enhance HSC survival and self-renewal, but will perturb normal differentiation dynamics, leading to an accumulation of primitive progenitors.
proposals.method: We will generate a novel mouse model with HSC-specific, inducible Cre-mediated deletion of Fas and overexpression of a human MCL1 transgene. Bone marrow from these mice and littermate controls will be analyzed using comprehensive flow cytometry panels (Lin-, Sca-1+, c-Kit+ [LSK] with CD150, CD48, CD34) to quantify HSC and progenitor populations. Competitive transplantation assays into lethally irradiated recipient mice will assess long-term repopulating capacity. Differentiation will be evaluated by colony-forming unit assays and peripheral blood lineage analysis. Apoptosis sensitivity will be tested ex vivo using Fas ligand and BH3 mimetics.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the specific, combined role of two key apoptotic regulators in HSC biology. It will reveal whether blocking extrinsic death while bolstering intrinsic survival creates a permissive niche-independent signal for HSC expansion. The findings could inform novel ex vivo HSC expansion protocols and provide mechanistic insight into leukemogenesis driven by similar pathway alterations.
proposals.novelty: While the individual roles of Fas and Mcl-1 are known, their coordinated, genetic manipulation specifically within the HSC compartment to dissect their synergistic impact on stem cell fate is a novel approach. This moves beyond single-pathway inhibition to model the complex, multi-faceted apoptotic resistance likely required for HSC persistence and transformation.

proposals.title: In Vivo Clonal Fate Mapping of Apoptosis-Resistant B Cells Reveals Compensatory Death Pathways and Lymphoid Homeostasis
proposals.motivation: The extrinsic (Fas/FasL) and intrinsic (Mcl-1-regulated) apoptosis pathways are critical for B cell homeostasis and tolerance, but their functional interplay in vivo remains unclear. While in vitro studies show redundancy, the fate of B cell clones doubly protected from apoptosis in a physiological setting is unknown, hindering our understanding of immune regulation and lymphomagenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the absence of Fas and with overexpression of Mcl-1, B cell clones will exhibit prolonged survival and altered differentiation fates in vivo, but that compensatory, Apaf-1- and Caspase-8-independent death pathways will ultimately constrain their expansion to maintain overall lymphoid homeostasis.
proposals.method: We will generate compound mutant mice (lpr/lpr; Mcl-1 Tg) with B cell-specific, tamoxifen-inducible Cre recombinase and a Confetti reporter (Rosa26-Confetti) for multicolor lineage tracing. Individual B cell clones will be tracked longitudinally in secondary lymphoid organs via multiphoton intravital microscopy and flow cytometry. Clonal dynamics (size, lifespan, differentiation into germinal center or memory cells) will be quantified and compared to controls (wild-type, lpr/lpr, Mcl-1 Tg) using established computational tools for lineage analysis (e.g., based on Fluorescent Ubiquitination-based Cell Cycle Indicator (FUCCI) and proliferation history markers).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly visualize the lifespan and fate decisions of apoptosis-resistant B cell clones in their native microenvironment. It will define the hierarchy and redundancy of apoptotic pathways in vivo and identify the compensatory mechanisms that prevent pathological clonal outgrowth, offering new insights into immune surveillance and B cell malignancies.
proposals.novelty: While apoptosis pathways have been studied biochemically and in bulk populations, applying high-resolution, multicolor genetic fate-mapping from developmental biology to track individual, genetically defined apoptotic-resistant immune clones in vivo is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond static snapshots to dynamic, clonal-level analysis of immune cell death.

proposals.title: Systemic consequences of hematopoietic apoptosis inhibition: Investigating non-hematopoietic tissue dysfunction via cytokine and cell-contact signaling
proposals.motivation: The Nagata lab has defined key apoptotic pathways (FasL, caspase-activated DNase) and their inhibitors, primarily in hematopoietic contexts. Pre-2026 clinical efforts to inhibit apoptosis (e.g., in sepsis or liver injury) focus on direct tissue protection, but the systemic effects of long-term apoptotic blockade in the immune compartment are unknown. This is critical because hematopoietic cells, through FasL expression and cytokine release, are known to regulate tissue homeostasis and inflammation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dual inhibition of Apaf-1- and Caspase-8-dependent apoptosis in hematopoietic cells disrupts immune homeostasis, leading to the accumulation of aberrant immune populations that indirectly induce apoptosis or functional impairment in non-hematopoietic tissues (e.g., liver, endothelium) via soluble factors or altered cell-cell contacts.
proposals.method: We will generate hematopoietic-specific double-knockout mice (Vav-Cre; Apaf-1 fl/fl; Caspase-8 fl/fl) to inhibit both intrinsic and extrinsic apoptosis. Tissue function will be assessed via serum transaminases (liver), albuminuria (kidney), and histology. Apoptosis in non-hematopoietic tissues will be quantified by TUNEL and cleaved caspase-3 IHC. To dissect mechanisms, we will perform cytokine array profiling from serum and co-culture experiments where bone marrow-derived macrophages from knockout mice are incubated with primary hepatocytes or endothelial cells, measuring target cell death and using neutralizing antibodies against candidate cytokines (e.g., TNF-α, IFN-γ) and FasL.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paradigm where apoptosis inhibition in one compartment has distal pathological consequences, moving beyond cell-autonomous effects. It will identify specific immune-derived signals that can damage tissues, informing the risks and potential side-effects of therapeutic apoptosis inhibitors intended for systemic use.
proposals.novelty: While apoptosis pathways are well-studied in isolation, the systemic, non-cell-autonomous consequences of their combined inhibition specifically in the hematopoietic system are unexplored. This links the lab's core expertise in apoptosis mechanisms to the emerging field of inter-tissue communication in disease.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Transcriptional Regulome of Fas Ligand in Activated Human T Lymphocytes
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL) is a critical death factor for immune homeostasis and cytotoxicity, but its dysregulation contributes to autoimmune diseases and immune escape in cancer. While FasL expression is known to be induced upon T cell activation, the precise, cell-type-specific transcriptional program governing its expression, compared to other FasL-expressing cells like NK cells or stromal cells, remains poorly defined. Identifying these specific regulators is essential for developing targeted immunomodulatory therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distinct set of transcription factors (TFs), activated downstream of the T cell receptor and co-stimulatory signals, binds to specific cis-regulatory elements to drive FasL expression selectively in activated T lymphocytes, differing from the regulatory logic in other immune or non-immune cells.
proposals.method: We will use primary human CD4+ and CD8+ T cells activated via anti-CD3/CD28. Chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) will be performed for histone marks (H3K27ac, H3K4me3) to map active promoters and enhancers at the FASLG locus. Putative regulatory regions will be screened using a CRISPR activation/interference (CRISPRa/i) library targeting candidate TFs (e.g., NFAT, AP-1, NF-κB, Egr family). Validated TFs will be subjected to ChIP-seq to confirm direct binding. Parallel RNA-seq after TF knockdown will define the FasL-specific transcriptional network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of the cell-type-specific transcriptional regulome controlling FasL in human T cells. It will identify key TFs and cis-elements responsible for activation-induced expression, offering new molecular targets for modulating Fas/FasL-mediated apoptosis in pathological contexts. The findings will advance our fundamental understanding of death receptor ligand regulation in adaptive immunity.
proposals.novelty: While FasL inducers are known, this study systematically identifies the *specific combination* of TFs and *cis-regulatory architecture* exclusive to activated T cells, moving beyond phenomenological observation to a mechanistic, genome-level understanding distinct from prior work on general apoptosis pathways.

proposals.title: Transcriptional and Post-Translational Determinants of Pro-Apoptotic Fas Ligand Expression in Human T Cells and NK Cells
proposals.motivation: While the regulation of Fas receptor expression and downstream signaling in target cells has been extensively studied, the factors that govern a cell's decision to express and present functional Fas ligand (FasL) remain poorly understood. This 'sender' cell problem is critical in immune homeostasis, cytotoxicity, and pathologies like autoimmune disorders and immune privilege. A systematic investigation into FasL regulation is needed to complement the established paradigm of Fas sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, inducible transcriptional programs and post-translational modifications, such as metalloproteinase-mediated shedding, are the primary determinants conferring a 'FasL sender' phenotype to human cytotoxic lymphocytes.
proposals.method: We will utilize primary human T cells and NK cells from healthy donors and patients with autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome (ALPS). FasL expression dynamics will be analyzed via RNA-seq and ATAC-seq upon activation (anti-CD3/CD28, IL-2, IL-15). A CRISPR-Cas9 screen in Jurkat T cells will target known and predicted transcription factors (e.g., NFAT, NF-κB, Egr family) to identify regulators of FasL promoter activity. Concurrently, we will profile FasL surface presentation versus shedding using flow cytometry with conformation-specific antibodies and ELISA for soluble FasL, employing specific metalloproteinase inhibitors (e.g., TAPI-2) to define the cleavage landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a regulatory map for FasL expression, identifying key transcription factors and cleavage events that define a FasL sender cell. It will provide a foundational framework for understanding how the immune system controls this potent death signal and may reveal novel therapeutic targets for modulating FasL-dependent immunopathology.
proposals.novelty: This proposal shifts the focus from the well-studied 'receiving' cell to the under-investigated 'sending' cell, systematically dissecting the positive regulatory mechanisms of FasL expression rather than the inhibitory checkpoints on Fas signaling.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Novel Binding Partners for the 40-kD Fas Ligand Protein
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL) is a critical mediator of apoptosis and immune homeostasis, and its interaction with the Fas receptor is well-established. However, the biological complexity of immune regulation and cell death signaling suggests that key ligands often have multiple receptors or binding partners. A comprehensive, unbiased search for novel FasL interactors is crucial to fully understand its physiological and pathological roles, particularly in immune privilege and inflammatory diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 40-kD FasL protein, beyond its canonical receptor Fas (CD95), interacts with one or more additional cell-surface or soluble proteins that modulate its apoptotic or non-apoptotic signaling functions.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged proteomics approach using pre-2026 technology. First, we will perform affinity purification mass spectrometry (AP-MS) using recombinant, biotinylated human FasL as bait to pull down interacting proteins from lysates of primary human T cells and sensitive tumor cell lines. Second, we will implement a cell-surface protein interaction screening using a library of human Fc-tagged receptor ectodomains immobilized on a protein array chip, probing for direct binding to labeled FasL. Identified candidates will be validated by co-immunoprecipitation and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to determine binding kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the FasL interactome, potentially revealing new receptors or decoy proteins that fine-tune apoptosis or transduce non-canonical signals. It could explain context-dependent sensitivities to FasL-induced death and identify new therapeutic targets for modulating immune responses in autoimmunity or cancer.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focused on Fas as the primary death-inducing receptor, this proposal systematically tests the long-standing assumption that Fas is the sole significant receptor for the 40-kD FasL, using modern, unbiased interaction proteomics not previously applied to this specific question.

proposals.title: Regulation of Fas Ligand Expression in Human Cytotoxic T Lymphocytes: Distinguishing Constitutive from Activation-Dependent Pathways
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL) is a key effector molecule for cytotoxic T lymphocyte (CTL)-mediated apoptosis, but its expression dynamics remain controversial. While some studies suggest FasL is stored intracellularly and rapidly mobilized upon activation, others indicate it is transcriptionally upregulated. Resolving this is critical for understanding immune regulation and pathologies like autoimmune disorders and immune evasion by tumors, where dysregulated Fas/FasL signaling plays a central role.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that human CTLs maintain a constitutive, intracellular pool of FasL, which is rapidly deployed to the cell surface upon T cell receptor (TCR) engagement, and that de novo transcription is required for sustained surface expression during prolonged activation.
proposals.method: We will isolate primary human CD8+ T cells from healthy donors and patients with autoimmune conditions. Using multi-parameter flow cytometry with specific anti-human FasL antibodies, we will quantify surface and intracellular FasL at rest and after stimulation via anti-CD3/CD28 or antigen-presenting cells. We will employ quantitative RT-PCR to measure FasL mRNA kinetics and use protein transport inhibitors (Brefeldin A, Monensin) and transcriptional blockers (Actinomycin D) to dissect the contributions of pre-formed protein versus new synthesis. Apoptotic function will be assessed in co-culture assays with Fas-expressing target cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the regulatory pathways controlling FasL expression in human CTLs, clarifying a long-standing mechanistic question. It will provide a foundational model for how rapid versus sustained cytotoxic potential is achieved, with direct implications for understanding immune homeostasis and designing therapies that modulate CTL activity.
proposals.novelty: This study directly tests the constitutive versus inducible expression models in primary human cells, integrating protein localization, transcriptional regulation, and functional output, moving beyond prior conflicting reports often based on cell lines or murine systems.

proposals.title: Molecular Cloning and Functional Expression of the Human Fas Ligand cDNA
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL) is a critical death factor that triggers apoptosis by cross-linking its receptor, Fas, playing essential roles in immune regulation, lymphocyte homeostasis, and cytotoxic T lymphocyte killing. Despite the identification of the FasL protein and its functional significance, the molecular cloning of its cDNA remains unachieved, hindering detailed mechanistic studies and the development of recombinant tools to modulate this pathway for therapeutic purposes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cDNA encoding human FasL can be isolated from an appropriate cytotoxic T cell line using oligonucleotide probes designed from partial protein sequence data, and that the recombinant protein expressed from this cDNA will be functional and induce Fas-mediated apoptosis.
proposals.method: We will synthesize degenerate oligonucleotide probes based on available N-terminal and internal peptide sequences of purified FasL. A cDNA library will be constructed from poly(A)+ RNA of the human cytotoxic T cell line, Jurkat, stimulated with phorbol ester and ionomycin to enhance FasL expression. This library will be screened using the probes under low-stringency hybridization conditions. Positive clones will be sequenced in full. The full-length cDNA will be subcloned into mammalian expression vectors (e.g., pcDNA3) and transfected into COS-7 cells. Recombinant FasL function will be assayed by its ability to induce apoptosis in Fas-expressing target cells (e.g., WR19L cells expressing human Fas).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first molecular clone for human FasL, enabling the production of recombinant protein for structural and functional studies. It will establish a critical reagent to definitively analyze FasL gene expression, regulation, and signal transduction, and to explore its pathophysiological roles in human diseases characterized by dysregulated apoptosis.
proposals.novelty: While the existence and function of FasL are known, its cDNA has not been cloned. Successfully isolating it would be a foundational step, moving the field from protein-level studies to genetic and recombinant analysis, which is non-trivial given the likely low abundance of its mRNA.

proposals.title: Structural and Functional Mapping of the Fas-Fas Ligand Interaction Interface Using Saturation Mutagenesis and Functional Assays
proposals.motivation: The Fas-FasL interaction is a critical trigger for apoptosis in immune regulation and disease, yet a comprehensive, residue-level map of the binding interface governing both complex formation and signal transduction is incomplete. Pre-2026 structural models exist, but they lack systematic validation in a cellular context, limiting the rational design of therapeutics that could modulate this pathway in autoimmunity or cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, non-overlapping clusters of residues on Fas and FasL are responsible for initial high-affinity binding versus the conformational changes required for downstream DISC assembly and caspase-8 activation.
proposals.method: We will employ deep mutational scanning using a mammalian surface display library of human Fas, coupled with high-throughput sorting based on binding to recombinant FasL. Parallelly, we will generate a comprehensive alanine-scanning library of FasL and test it against wild-type Fas. Key binding mutants identified will be stably expressed in FADD-deficient cells to isolate binding from signaling. Their ability to initiate apoptosis will then be tested in reconstitution assays by introducing them into Fas-negative cells and stimulating with wild-type FasL, measuring caspase-8 activation and DNA fragmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a definitive functional epitope map, distinguishing binding residues from signaling-essential residues. It will provide a crucial resource for understanding natural mutations and for developing targeted agonists/antagonists. The data will also refine existing structural models by highlighting residues critical in a physiological membrane context.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies identified broad regions, this systematic, high-resolution functional scan in human cells is novel. Integrating quantitative binding measurements with specific apoptotic readouts will decouple these two events at a residue level, a distinction not achieved in prior work.

proposals.title: Mapping the Proximal Signaling Proteome of the Activated Fas Death-Inducing Signaling Complex (DISC) Using In Situ Chemical Crosslinking and Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: The Fas/FasL system is a critical pathway for apoptosis in immune regulation and disease. While core DISC components (FADD, caspase-8) are known, the full composition of the signaling complex, including potential regulatory or alternative signaling proteins, remains incompletely characterized. A comprehensive, proximity-based map is needed to understand the molecular basis of life/death decisions and pathological dysregulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in situ chemical crosslinking of the Fas receptor upon ligand engagement will capture novel, transient, and/or weak protein-protein interactions within the native DISC that are missed by conventional co-immunoprecipitation, revealing new regulators of Fas-mediated apoptosis.
proposals.method: We will treat human Jurkat T-cells or primary human lymphocytes with soluble, crosslinkable FasL. In situ crosslinking will be performed using membrane-permeable, amine-reactive crosslinkers (e.g., DSS) or photoreactive crosslinkers (e.g., Sulfo-SDA) at defined time points post-stimulation. The Fas complex will be isolated under stringent conditions via immunoprecipitation with anti-Fas antibodies. Crosslinked proteins will be identified and quantified using high-resolution tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) coupled with bioinformatic analysis using the StavroX software suite for crosslink identification and MaxQuant for label-free quantification.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first high-resolution, proximity-based interactome of the activated Fas receptor in living cells. It will likely identify novel DISC-associated proteins that modulate caspase-8 activation, potentially linking Fas to non-apoptotic pathways or revealing new therapeutic targets for autoimmune and lymphoproliferative disorders.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous studies relying on detergent-based co-IP, this approach uses in situ crosslinking to freeze transient and low-affinity interactions within the native membrane environment of the living cell, providing a more dynamic and physiologically relevant snapshot of the early Fas signaling complex.

proposals.title: Development and Application of a FLAG-Tagged Fas Ligand System for Enhanced Detection and Purification of Functional Apoptotic Signaling Complexes
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL) is a critical death-inducing cytokine central to immune regulation and disease. However, studying its native expression, processing, and interaction with the Fas receptor in human cells remains challenging due to low endogenous expression and a lack of high-affinity, non-interfering detection tools. Current antibodies can be limiting for biochemical purification and detailed analysis of the FasL-Fas signaling complex.
proposals.hypothesis: Engineering a C-terminally FLAG-tagged human FasL will yield a fully functional protein that can be efficiently expressed, detected with high sensitivity, and purified from cell membranes or supernatants without disrupting its apoptotic activity or receptor binding.
proposals.method: We will construct a mammalian expression vector encoding full-length human FasL with a C-terminal 3xFLAG tag. This construct will be transfected into HEK293T cells and activated T-cells to validate expression via anti-FLAG immunoblotting and immunofluorescence. Functionality will be confirmed by co-culture with Fas-expressing target cells and apoptosis assays (Annexin V/PI). For purification, membrane fractions from expressing cells will be solubilized in mild detergent, and FasL-FLAG will be isolated using anti-FLAG M2 affinity gel chromatography. Eluted complexes will be analyzed by silver staining and mass spectrometry to identify associated proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, widely applicable tool for the sensitive detection and biochemical isolation of FasL and its native signaling complexes. It will enable detailed studies of FasL post-translational modifications, regulated secretion, and the composition of the death-inducing signaling complex (DISC) in primary human immune cells, advancing our understanding of apoptotic signaling fidelity.
proposals.novelty: While epitope tagging is common, its application to create a functional, purifiable FasL tailored for studying native membrane-bound and soluble complexes in human immune cells is novel. This approach bypasses the limitations of existing FasL antibodies and offers a superior method for complex purification.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Characterization of Endogenous Soluble Fas Ligand Antagonists in Human Tissues and Plasma
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL)-mediated apoptosis is a critical regulator of immune homeostasis and cytotoxicity, but its dysregulation contributes to autoimmune diseases, graft rejection, and tumor immune evasion. While decoy receptors for other TNF family ligands (e.g., DcR3 for FasL is known but not widely expressed) are established, a comprehensive search for endogenous, physiologically relevant FasL antagonists in humans is lacking. Identifying such natural inhibitors could reveal novel mechanisms controlling immune cell death and provide new therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that human cells and plasma contain previously unidentified soluble proteins or splice variants that function as high-affinity antagonists of membrane-bound FasL, thereby acting as endogenous regulators of Fas-mediated apoptosis.
proposals.method: We will employ a functional proteomics screen using a recombinant human FasL-Fc chimera as bait to affinity-purify binding partners from primary human lymphocyte lysates and pooled healthy donor plasma. Candidate proteins will be identified by mass spectrometry. Binding specificity and affinity will be validated by surface plasmon resonance (Biacore). Functional antagonism will be tested in vitro using Jurkat T-cells and Fas-sensitive target cells, measuring apoptosis via flow cytometry (Annexin V/PI) and caspase-8 activation. We will also analyze RNA-seq datasets from public repositories (e.g., GTEx) for evidence of alternative splicing producing soluble decoy isoforms of known Fas-related genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proteomic landscape of endogenous FasL interactors, potentially identifying novel physiological inhibitors. It will advance our understanding of how Fas-mediated apoptosis is finely tuned in vivo. The discovery of such regulators could open avenues for developing biologics that modulate Fas signaling in autoimmune or inflammatory conditions.
proposals.novelty: This systematic search moves beyond the known decoy receptor DcR3 and the soluble FasL isoform itself, seeking novel endogenous protein antagonists in primary human material, a direct approach not previously undertaken in the context of Nagata's foundational work on FasL-induced apoptosis.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Quantification of Fas Ligand Expression Correlates with Cytotoxic T Lymphocyte Killing Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Fas ligand (FasL) is a critical effector molecule for cytotoxic T lymphocyte (CTL)-mediated apoptosis, but its expression and functional heterogeneity at the single-cell level remain poorly characterized. Bulk analyses mask the variability between individual CTLs, limiting our understanding of what defines a highly effective 'killer' cell. This gap is crucial for deciphering immune responses in cancer, autoimmunity, and viral infections where CTL function is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the level of FasL expression in individual human CTLs directly correlates with their intrinsic capacity to induce apoptosis in Fas-expressing target cells, and that this relationship is modulated by the co-expression of other key regulatory proteins like the caspase-8 inhibitor FLIP.
proposals.method: We will isolate antigen-specific human CTLs from patient-derived samples or in vitro cultures. Using high-parameter flow cytometry (e.g., 15+ colors) and index sorting, we will simultaneously measure surface and intracellular proteins (FasL, perforin, granzymes, FLIP, activation markers) and subsequently sort single cells into 96-well plates. Single-cell RT-PCR will then quantify mRNA levels for FASLG and related genes (e.g., CFLAR for FLIP). The killing capacity of phenotypically defined subsets will be functionally validated in real-time using co-culture assays with fluorescently labeled, Fas-expressing target cells and imaging flow cytometry to track apoptosis initiation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, single-cell map linking FasL expression to CTL effector function, moving beyond binary assessments of presence/absence. It will identify molecular signatures of high-efficacy CTLs and reveal how regulatory molecules like FLIP fine-tune the Fas pathway at the single-cell level, providing a refined framework for understanding immune dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While FasL's role in apoptosis is well-established, directly correlating its precise expression levels at the single-cell level with quantitative killing outcomes in primary human CTLs is novel. Integrating high-parameter protein indexing with single-cell gene expression and functional validation for this pathway represents a significant technical and conceptual advance over prior bulk population studies.

proposals.title: Transcriptional and Post-translational Control of Fas Ligand Expression in Human T Cells: Identifying Novel Upstream Signaling Pathways
proposals.motivation: While Fas ligand (FasL)-induced apoptosis is a critical mechanism for immune homeostasis and cytotoxicity, research has predominantly focused on its downstream signaling. The specific upstream signals that transcriptionally upregulate or post-translationally stabilize FasL in human immune cells remain incompletely defined. A systematic investigation is needed to understand how immune activation and microenvironmental cues precisely control the expression of this potent death effector, with implications for autoimmunity, cancer immunotherapy, and lymphoproliferative disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific T cell receptor (TCR) co-stimulatory signals and cytokine milieus activate distinct, non-redundant transcriptional programs and post-translational modifications that converge to regulate FasL expression and surface presentation in human CD4+ and CD8+ T cells.
proposals.method: We will use primary human T cells from healthy donors and patients with autoimmune conditions. Transcriptional regulation will be dissected using TCR/CD28 stimulation under defined cytokine conditions (e.g., IL-2, IL-12, IFN-γ), followed by chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) for known (NFAT, NF-κB, Egr) and candidate transcription factors, coupled with RNA-seq and luciferase reporter assays of the FASLG promoter/enhancer. Post-translational control will be analyzed via phospho-proteomics and surface biotinylation assays upon stimulation to track FasL protein stability, shedding, and membrane trafficking. Key findings will be validated using specific kinase inhibitors and CRISPR-Cas9-mediated gene knockout in primary T cells.
proposals.contribution: This study will map the integrated signaling network controlling FasL expression, moving beyond the phenomenological observation of its induction. It will identify novel regulatory nodes that could be targeted to modulate FasL-dependent apoptosis. The findings will provide a foundational regulatory framework for understanding pathological FasL dysregulation in human diseases.
proposals.novelty: Previous work by Nagata and others established FasL's apoptotic function and basic inducers, but a comprehensive, mechanistic dissection of the upstream signaling logic in primary human T cells is lacking. This proposal uniquely combines systematic transcriptomic, epigenomic, and proteomic analyses to deconvolve these specific regulatory pathways.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Dominant Immunoregulatory Function of the Fas/FasL System: A Focus on Peripheral Deletion versus T Cell Exhaustion in Autoimmunity
proposals.motivation: The Fas/FasL system is a canonical death receptor pathway critical for immune homeostasis, and its dysfunction leads to lymphoproliferation and autoimmunity, as seen in lpr and gld mice. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests Fas can also induce non-apoptotic signaling, and its role in activation-induced cell death (AICD) may be context-dependent. It remains unclear whether Fas-mediated deletion of chronically activated T cells is its primary physiological function or if it serves a more dominant role in modulating T cell exhaustion or anergy to prevent immunopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary immunoregulatory function of the Fas/FasL system in mature T cells is not the acute deletion of recently activated cells (AICD), but rather the enforcement of a dysfunctional or 'exhausted' state in chronically stimulated T cells, thereby limiting autoimmunity through mechanisms beyond simple cell death.
proposals.method: We will use Fas-deficient (lpr) and FasL-deficient (gld) mice on a C57BL/6 background. To model chronic stimulation, we will employ the LCMV clone 13 chronic infection model and a repeated self-antigen exposure model using transgenic T cells. We will analyze antigen-specific CD8+ and CD4+ T cell populations by high-dimensional flow cytometry (30+ parameters) for surface markers of exhaustion (PD-1, LAG-3, TIM-3), activation, and memory. Apoptosis will be quantified by Annexin V/7-AAD and active caspase-3 staining. We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq, 10x Genomics platform) on sorted antigen-specific T cells from wild-type and mutant mice to define transcriptional programs. Functional assays will measure cytokine polyfunctionality (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2) after restimulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will clarify the dominant physiological function of the Fas pathway in peripheral T cell tolerance. It will determine whether Fas deficiency primarily leads to autoimmunity due to failed deletion or due to an accumulation of dysfunctional, yet pathogenic, T cell clones. The findings will refine our understanding of death receptor biology in immune regulation.
proposals.novelty: While Fas is well-studied in apoptosis, systematically testing if its non-deletional role in enforcing T cell exhaustion is its primary function in vivo is novel. This shifts the paradigm from viewing Fas mainly as an 'executioner' to a key modulator of T cell functional states.

proposals.title: Systematic Profiling of Target Cell Susceptibility to Double-Negative T Cell Cytotoxicity in the lpr Mouse Model
proposals.motivation: In MRL/lpr mice, a model of human lupus, the accumulation of CD4-CD8- (double-negative, DN) T cells correlates with autoimmunity. While these cells are known to be cytotoxic, their precise target cell repertoire and the molecular pathways governing their killing specificity remain poorly defined. Elucidating this is critical for understanding their pathogenic role in autoimmunity versus potential regulatory functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that activated DN T cells from lpr mice exhibit a broad but distinct cytotoxicity profile against various immune and non-immune cell types, utilizing both Fas/FasL-dependent and independent apoptotic pathways.
proposals.method: We will isolate and activate splenic DN T cells from MRL/lpr mice. A panel of target cells, including syngeneic lymphocytes (B, CD4+, CD8+ T cells), dendritic cells, macrophages, and epithelial cell lines, will be co-cultured with DN T cells. Cytotoxicity will be quantified via standard 51Cr-release and Annexin V/PI flow cytometry assays. Specificity will be dissected using blocking antibodies against FasL, TRAIL, and perforin/granzyme pathways, and by employing target cells deficient in Fas (lpr-derived) or caspase-8.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of target cell susceptibility to lpr-derived DN T cell killing, directly linking their cytotoxic potential to specific death receptor/ligand systems. It will clarify whether DN T cell cytotoxicity is promiscuous or selective, informing models of how they drive tissue damage in lupus.
proposals.novelty: Previous work focused on DN T cell ontogeny and general phenotype. This proposal systematically defines their functional target spectrum and mechanistically disentangles the specific apoptotic pathways (FasL vs. others) involved, building directly on Nagata's foundational work in Fas-induced and caspase-8-independent apoptosis.

ideas.title: Engineer and validate a synthetic logic gate framework for Fas-mediated apoptosis susceptibility.
ideas.description: This project moves beyond observation to establish causal design principles for tissue-specific apoptotic regulation. We will engineer a modular, synthetic Fas signaling pathway in cells from resistant and sensitive tissues, allowing independent control of key regulatory nodes. By systematically reconstituting hypothesized resistance mechanisms in sensitive cells and disabling them in resistant cells, we can rigorously test their sufficiency and necessity. This synthetic approach will define the minimal regulatory logic required to switch a tissue's apoptotic fate, providing a foundational framework for predicting pathological sensitivity and designing targeted immunomodulatory therapies.

ideas.title: Correlate BCR signal dynamics with caspase substrate cleavage to define apoptotic fate decisions in B cell subsets
ideas.description: This study will use single-cell calcium imaging to capture the kinetic profiles of BCR signaling in distinct B cell subsets (e.g., RP105+ vs. CD40-stimulated blasts) and then, in the same cells, induce Fas-mediated apoptosis. By applying a systematic proteomics approach (e.g., N-terminomics) to these pre-profiled populations, we will catalog the complete set of caspase substrates specifically cleaved in cells that exhibited defined early calcium signatures. This integrated methodology will determine whether subtle differences in early BCR signal amplitude or duration are functionally encoded into divergent apoptotic proteolytic programs, potentially identifying novel caspase substrates whose cleavage is contingent on prior activation history.

ideas.title: Mapping the Context-Dependent Logic of Apoptotic Commitment and Escape
ideas.description: This project systematically tests the linearity and irreversibility of apoptosis across a diverse landscape of cellular contexts. Using single-cell functional genomics, it perturbs key apoptotic nodes in multiple cell types exposed to different death stimuli while monitoring real-time commitment dynamics. The goal is to construct a predictive map of which cellular conditions permit feedback, reversal, or non-engagement of the canonical cascade, moving beyond a binary test of a single pathway model.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporally controlled pathway blockade to map the transition from compensatory proliferation to oncogenic hyperplasia
ideas.description: This research program combines the acute, pathway-specific inhibitory precision of dominant-negative mutants with the phenotypic analysis of apoptotic loss-of-function to investigate causal dynamics in tissue homeostasis. By introducing inducible dominant-negative constructs against key apoptotic signaling molecules in specific cell populations, we can precisely initiate the block and then characterize the ensuing cellular consequences in real-time. This allows us to map the direct transition from inhibited apoptosis to early compensatory proliferation and, critically, to determine the point at which this becomes dysregulated, pathological hyperplasia. The approach reveals not just that apoptosis loss causes proliferation, but which specific blocked pathway nodes and downstream signaling cascades are necessary to drive the shift from normal tissue repair to pre-oncogenic state.

ideas.title: Live-cell mapping of tissue-specific apoptotic signaling nodes using multiplexed FRET biosensors in primary immune cells
ideas.description: This project combines the mechanistic question of tissue-specific apoptotic regulation with the live-cell imaging power of FRET biosensors to create a novel experimental platform. We will engineer and deploy a panel of FRET biosensors targeting critical nodes in the Fas signaling pathway (e.g., receptor aggregation, DISC formation, caspase-8 cleavage) and apply them to primary B cells isolated from different lymphoid (e.g., spleen, lymph nodes) and non-lymphoid tissues. By stimulating these spatially distinct B cell populations with Fas ligand while simultaneously monitoring multiple biosensor readouts in real time, we can directly correlate specific protein interaction dynamics with cell fate decisions. This approach will reveal how the kinetic architecture of the apoptotic signaling network varies by tissue microenvironment to produce differential sensitivity or resistance.

ideas.title: Apoptosis as a Cybernetic System: Testing for Feedback and Reversibility
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that apoptosis is a strictly linear, irreversible cascade by drawing an analogy from control systems engineering. In that field, the linearity and irreversibility of a process are tested by perturbing the system and observing if it can return to its original state, and by modeling to identify feedback mechanisms. The adapted research idea is to treat the apoptotic cascade as a potential cybernetic system. This involves designing biological experiments to perturb key apoptotic nodes (e.g., with caspase inhibitors, pro-survival signal surges, or mitochondrial recovery agents) at different stages to test for points of reversal or attenuation. Concurrently, computational models should be built to identify potential, yet undiscovered, negative feedback loops within the canonical pathway that could make the process non-linear and regulable, rather than a simple domino effect.

ideas.title: Mapping the Reversibility of Microenvironment-Imposed Apoptotic Blockade in B Cell Fate
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether survival signals from the stromal microenvironment can not only override pro-apoptotic RP105/BCR engagement but can actively reverse specific nodes within the initiated apoptotic cascade. It moves beyond testing linearity by asking if microenvironmental cues (e.g., specific cytokines) can rewind execution-phase events like mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP) or caspase activation in B cells, and defines the critical time window and molecular checkpoints for such reversibility. The core synergy is that it uses the specific molecular conflict (RP105/BCR vs. stroma) as a precise tool to trigger and then probe the plasticity of the apoptotic pathway itself, revealing if cell fate is a point-of-no-return decision or a continuously adjustable state governed by extracellular signaling.

ideas.title: Elucidate T cell-help-dependent CD40 signaling networks that confer BCR-apoptosis resistance in germinal center B cells
ideas.description: This project aims to define the molecular circuitry activated by physiological CD40 signaling—delivered via T follicular helper cells—that protects activated B cells from BCR-induced apoptosis. Using primary mouse or human B and T cell co-cultures, we will perform temporal phosphoproteomic profiling to map signaling dynamics and couple this with context-specific functional genomics screens to identify essential nodes within the protective network. The physiological relevance of identified pathways will be rigorously tested by disrupting them in B cells and assessing competitive fitness and apoptosis sensitivity during germinal center responses in vivo.

ideas.title: Testing the Context-Dependent Role of FcγRIIB in RP105 Apoptosis Using a Neurotransmitter Receptor Agonist/Allosteric Modulator Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the uniform assumption of FcγRIIB's role in augmenting RP105-mediated apoptosis by drawing an analogy to solved problems in neuropharmacology, specifically the concept of 'biased agonism' in G-Protein Coupled Receptor (GPCR) signaling. In GPCRs, different ligands can stabilize unique receptor conformations, leading to distinct and sometimes opposing downstream effects (functional selectivity). The analogous solution involved systematically testing different agonists and mapping their specific signaling pathways. Adapted to this immunological context, the research idea is to treat different antibody isotypes or ligand densities not merely as strengths of signal but as distinct 'biased ligands' for the FcγRIIB/RP105 complex. The adapted experiment would use a panel of engineered engagement molecules to test the hypothesis that the functional outcome of FcγRIIB coligation (strong augmentation, weak augmentation, or even inhibition of apoptosis) is not uniform but is determined by the specific molecular mode of receptor engagement, thereby revealing a spectrum of context-dependent regulatory functions for FcγRIIB.

ideas.title: Chromatin Catabolites as Active Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns (DAMPs)
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that chromosomal DNA degradation is a terminal, passive event. Drawing an analogy from immunology's Danger Signal Theory and the established role of Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns (DAMPs), the project posits that specific DNA degradation products may act as active signaling molecules. The analogous solution—identifying endogenous damage-derived molecules that bind specific receptors to alert the organism—is adapted to investigate whether defined chromatin fragments (e.g., nucleosome-sized DNA, CpG-rich oligonucleotides, or 8-oxoguanine-containing fragments) released during cell death are recognized by receptors (like TLR9, AIM2, or others) on adjacent cells. The goal is to determine if these 'chromatin catabolites' actively transmit information about cell death type, location, or extent, thereby coordinating tissue-level responses such as immune recruitment, compensatory proliferation, or fibrosis, transforming our view of DNA breakdown from a cleanup process to a communicative one.

ideas.title: Dynamic Signaling Cartography to Reprogram Apoptosis Resistance in B Cell Lymphomas
ideas.description: This research direction begins with a panel of primary B cell lymphomas clinically resistant to BCR-induced apoptosis. It then employs high-throughput phospho-flow cytometry to simultaneously quantify the dynamic signaling states of key nodes (e.g., NF-κB, MAPK, PI3K/AKT) downstream of BCR, RP105, and CD40, both individually and in combination, within each resistant cell. By correlating these high-dimensional signaling profiles with functional apoptosis outcomes after receptor modulation, the study will identify the specific signaling bottlenecks or compensatory pathways that maintain resistance. This allows for the iterative testing of tailored receptor engagement sequences designed to overcome these bottlenecks, moving beyond simple susceptibility testing to actively engineer a permissive signaling network for cell death.

ideas.title: Characterizing the 'Runaway Train' Phenotype: Applying Control Theory to Map the Systemic Consequences of Apoptotic Failure
ideas.description: This research seed aims to characterize hyper-proliferation resulting from apoptotic mutations. Drawing an analogy from Control Systems Engineering, where the failure of a critical component like a brake is analyzed through system-wide dynamic modeling, this project adapts that solution. Instead of focusing solely on the molecular details of the broken apoptotic pathway, the adapted idea treats the cell as a complex control network. The goal is to create an integrated, quantitative model that simulates how loss-of-function in apoptotic molecules disrupts interconnected feedback loops with cell cycle checkpoints, DNA repair mechanisms, and metabolic regulators. This systems-level approach will predict the cascading physiological consequences—the 'runaway train' phenotype—and identify potential compensatory mechanisms or synthetic lethal interactions that could be targeted therapeutically, moving beyond a static pathway analysis to a dynamic understanding of apoptotic failure.

ideas.title: Decoding the Stromal 'Safety Net': A Network Resilience Approach to B-Cell Survival Signaling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of network resilience from ecology to investigate how survival signals from the microenvironment override pro-apoptotic BCR engagement. In ecology, a node's survival under attack depends on redundant support pathways within its network. The analogous solution involves mapping these alternative connections to analyze compensatory capacity. Adapted to immunology, the B-cell is treated as a network node where RP105/BCR engagement is a primary pro-apoptotic stressor. The research will systematically map and quantify the parallel, pro-survival signaling edges (e.g., specific cytokine signals, adhesion-mediated signals, metabolic support) originating from stromal cells in the microenvironment. The goal is to model this as a network switching problem, identifying the critical combination, hierarchy, and intensity of these 'alternative support pathways' that collectively build a resilient 'safety net' sufficient to flip the cell's fate from death to survival.

ideas.title: In vivo causal dissection of death factor crosstalk governing cell fate decisions
ideas.description: This project moves beyond descriptive mapping to establish causal, physiologically relevant mechanisms of death pathway integration. Using a transgenic mouse model enabling cell-type-specific, inducible CRISPR knockout or activation of key nodes (e.g., Bcl-2 family members, caspase-8, MLKL), we perturb the system in vivo during tissue turnover or in response to defined injury. High-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing and multiplexed tissue imaging of the perturbed tissues will then reveal the precise transcriptional and phenotypic consequences of disrupting specific crosstalk points. This approach directly tests how death factor signaling networks are wired in vivo to produce integrated life/death decisions, identifying context-specific regulatory hubs that could be therapeutic targets.

ideas.title: Applying Plant Grafting Compatibility Logic to Human B Cell Receptor Dichotomy
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from plant biology, specifically the solved problem of predicting graft compatibility between different plant species based on conserved signaling pathways. The analogous solution involved identifying universal molecular markers of compatibility in model systems before application to complex, valuable crops. Adapted to immunology, the research will first map the core, conserved intracellular signaling network (the 'compatibility scaffold') that creates the RP105/CD40 apoptosis dichotomy in established mouse B cell models. This conserved network will then serve as the testable framework for human scaling. The investigation will determine if this network is identically wired in human B cells from healthy donors and, crucially, if malfunctions in these specific nodes are present in B cells from patients with autoimmune diseases like SLE or RA, thereby linking the conserved mechanism directly to clinical relevance in autoimmunity.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Fate Mapping of Human B Cell Dysregulation in Autoimmunity
ideas.description: This research direction employs high-content, long-term live-cell imaging of primary human B cells from healthy donors and patients with autoimmune diseases (e.g., SLE, RA) to track the fate decisions of individual cells following RP105 or CD40 co-stimulation with BCR engagement. By integrating this single-cell temporal data with deep immunophenotyping and patient clinical metadata, the study will identify distinct, aberrant fate decision trajectories (e.g., failed apoptosis, biased differentiation) that correlate with disease activity or specific autoantibody profiles. This approach moves beyond conservation to establish a causal, mechanistic link between receptor-specific signaling dynamics and the breakdown of tolerance in specific human autoimmune contexts.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismometer Array Principles for Multi-Caspase Network Mapping in Live Tissue
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where arrays of seismometers map earthquake dynamics, this idea adapts the principle for cell biology. The analogous solution uses a sensor network to localize and characterize propagating waves of activity. The adapted research idea is to employ a multiplexed array of spectrally distinct FRET-based biosensors targeting different caspases (e.g., initiators and executioners) within live cells organized in a 3D tissue model. This sensor network will enable the visualization of apoptosis not as a single event, but as a propagating signaling wave, allowing researchers to determine the spatial origin, sequence of protease activation, and directionality of cell death spread in real-time.

ideas.title: Adapting Allosteric Inhibitors from Pharmacology to Block Apoptotic Pathway Hubs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the pharmacological concept of allosteric inhibition to the method of using dominant-negative mutants. In drug discovery, allosteric inhibitors provide high specificity by binding regulatory sites to disrupt protein function. The analogous solution is to use structural knowledge to design signaling protein mutants that are not merely binding-deficient but are conformationally locked in an inactive state, thereby disrupting the entire downstream complex in a dominant-negative fashion. Adapted to the research context, we engineer such allosteric-dominant mutants for key hubs (e.g., in the caspase cascade or upstream kinase pathways) and introduce them to test the absolute necessity of signal propagation through that specific node for the differential apoptotic fate. This refines the original method by targeting functional integrity rather than just ligand-receptor competition, offering a more nuanced tool for pathway dissection.

ideas.title: Optogenetic Dissection of the BCR-Stroma Signaling Integration Node Controlling B Cell Fate
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlation to establish causal mechanism by using orthogonal, tunable actuators to map how survival signals override BCR-induced apoptosis. An optogenetic BCR (optoBCR) provides precise spatiotemporal control of the pro-apoptotic signal, while defined stromal factors or chemogenetic actuators deliver competing pro-survival signals. By systematically varying the timing, strength, and pathway specificity of these inputs and monitoring integrated downstream effectors in real time, the research will define the critical signaling nodes and decision thresholds that determine cell fate. This approach will generate a predictive, quantitative model of the signaling network that governs B cell survival in the microenvironment.

ideas.title: A Network Topology Approach to Death Factor Crosstalk
ideas.description: This research adapts methodologies from urban traffic engineering and network theory to investigate crosstalk between apoptotic death factor pathways. The analogous problem—understanding how multiple traffic flows integrate and interact at complex intersections to determine overall traffic patterns—is solved by mapping networks, identifying critical nodes, and modeling interaction rules. Adapted to cell biology, the idea involves constructing a detailed interaction map of death factor pathways (TNF, FasL, TRAIL, etc.) as a network graph. Computational models will then be used to simulate signal flow, identify key integration hubs (analogous to major traffic intersections), and deduce the logic gates (e.g., AND, OR, priority signals) that determine whether pro-apoptotic signals are amplified or suppressed when multiple pathways are activated. This approach aims to move beyond pairwise interactions and predict systems-level cell fate decisions in response to combinatorial death signals.

ideas.title: Map the RP105 signaling network using a phosphoproteomic and computational systems approach, analogous to kinase-substrate mapping in plant stress response
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from plant systems biology, where high-throughput phosphoproteomics and computational network inference are used to map signaling cascades downstream of specific receptors. The analogous problem of identifying kinase-substrate networks in plant stress response is solved by capturing global phosphorylation changes post-stimulation and using algorithms to reconstruct causal pathways. Adapted to this context, the research would involve stimulating B cells via RP105 ligation and performing time-course phosphoproteomic analysis. The resulting data would be fed into network inference and kinase-substrate prediction models to systematically identify the direct intracellular signaling pathways (e.g., specific MAPK, PI3K, or NF-κB modules) activated by RP105 that prime the cells for subsequent anti-IgM-induced apoptosis, moving beyond candidate molecule approaches to a systems-level map.

ideas.title: Investigate if BCR-induced DNA degradation products modulate calcium signaling in neighboring B cells
ideas.description: This fused idea proposes to use single-cell calcium imaging to monitor BCR-induced calcium flux in viable B cell blasts while exposing them to conditioned media or defined degradation products from B cells undergoing activation-induced cytidine deaminase (AID)-dependent DNA damage or apoptosis. The core hypothesis is that chromosomal DNA fragments released during B cell receptor engagement are not merely waste but act as signaling molecules that can prime or alter the calcium response kinetics in neighboring B cells. This approach directly tests if a 'passive' degradation event can actively modulate a key early activation signal, potentially revealing a novel feedback loop where DNA damage in one cell calibrates the activation threshold of its neighbors.

ideas.title: Quantifying the causal link between apoptotic loss-of-function and clonal expansion through inducible mutagenesis and lineage tracing.
ideas.description: This study moves beyond descriptive characterization to establish direct causal links by employing a dual-component experimental system. First, an inducible CRISPR/Cas9 system is used to generate loss-of-function mutations in key apoptotic signaling molecules within primary epithelial or hematopoietic cells. Second, these cells are endowed with a unique, heritable genetic barcode prior to mutagenesis, enabling high-resolution lineage tracing. By monitoring barcoded clonal populations over time in defined culture conditions, the system precisely quantifies the intrinsic hyper-proliferative capacity, survival advantage, and competitive fitness conferred by each mutation, independent of tissue microenvironment and organismal compensation.

ideas.title: Apply Network Failure Analysis from Power Grids to Gene Expression Profiles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from power grid failure analysis, where system-wide sensor data is used to model networks and identify critical failure points. The analogous solution involves using sensor data to model a power grid network to find critical nodes whose failure causes cascading collapse. Adapted to the research context, we will use cDNA microarray gene expression profiles from RP105 and CD40 blasts not merely to generate a list, but to construct a gene co-expression or regulatory interaction network. Applying network analysis algorithms (e.g., calculating betweenness centrality or degree) will identify key 'hub' genes whose expression patterns are most central and interconnected within the network specific to the apoptosis-susceptible phenotype. These predicted regulatory hubs, which may not be the most differentially expressed, are then prioritized as the master controllers dictating apoptosis susceptibility for experimental validation, moving from correlation to causal network inference.

ideas.title: Live-Cell Phospho-Flow Tracking of Single B Cell Fate Decisions
ideas.description: This project combines phospho-specific flow cytometry with live-cell imaging in a novel integrated platform. Individual B cells will be subjected to RP105, CD40, and/or BCR stimulation, and their real-time signaling network activity will be captured via repeated, non-destructive phospho-flow sampling at key time points. These high-dimensional phospho-profiles are then linked to the subsequent fate of each tracked cell—such as division, death, or differentiation—determined by time-lapse microscopy. This approach uniquely identifies the precise signaling signatures and dynamics that are predictive of specific cell fates, moving beyond population averages to establish causal maps between signaling networks and functional outcomes.

ideas.title: FLIM-FRET biosensors with internal calibration for quantitative protein interaction mapping in living B cells
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) to measure FRET, which is insensitive to fluorophore concentration and excitation intensity, directly overcoming autofluorescence and spectral bleed-through artifacts. Each B cell also expresses a cytosolic reference fluorophore not involved in FRET, allowing for ratiometric normalization of the biosensor expression level and correction for cell thickness and shape. This dual-calibration system enables precise, quantitative tracking of both strong and weak protein interaction dynamics in real time following B cell receptor stimulation, even in dense cell populations. The result is a robust, generalizable platform for mapping signaling networks with high spatiotemporal resolution in primary immune cells.

ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlative observations to mechanistically define the precise features of chromosomal DNA fragments that confer signaling capacity. Using synthetic DNA libraries and controlled release systems, it tests the sufficiency of specific fragment lengths, sequences, and modifications to activate known nucleic acid sensing pathways in recipient cells. The design directly compares these synthetic signals to degradation products from physiologically relevant cell death, isolating context from content. This approach will establish causal rules for when DNA degradation transitions from a passive waste product to an active intercellular signal.

ideas.title: Single-cell FRET biosensor tracking of early signaling dynamics to predict B cell fate decisions
ideas.description: This research direction combines FRET-based biosensors with single-cell time-lapse fate tracking to investigate how early signaling dynamics dictate B cell fate. By expressing FRET biosensors that report interactions within the BCR, RP105, or CD40 signaling complexes in individual primary B cells, we will quantify the precise kinetics and amplitude of these molecular events in real time following specific stimulations. Each profiled cell will then be tracked over days via time-lapse microscopy to correlate its unique early signaling signature—such as the timing of NF-κB activation or PI3K recruitment—with its eventual fate decision, including proliferation, differentiation, or death. This approach moves beyond correlation to establish predictive, mechanistic links between nanoscale protein interaction dynamics and macroscopic cellular outcomes.

ideas.title: Use inducible, orthogonal steric blockade to dissect pathway necessity with high specificity
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a synthetic biology strategy to achieve pathway-specific inhibition without dominant-negative off-target effects. A drug-inducible dimerization system is used to recruit a catalytically dead version of a pathway terminal effector to a scaffold protein localized at the site of active signaling complexes. This creates a precise, steric blockade that prevents native effector binding only at engaged receptors or adaptors, mimicking a genetic knockout within the complex. The system allows for temporal control and tests pathway necessity by observing if differential apoptotic fate is disrupted when the specific signaling node is physically occluded, minimizing pleiotropic interference.

ideas.title: A causal signaling map defining the integration node and functional logic of RP105 and CD40 inputs
ideas.description: This redesigned project moves beyond correlation to establish a causal, mechanistic model for how RP105 and CD40 signals are processed. It employs an integrated experimental pipeline combining omics-based pathway mapping with functional perturbation and synthetic biology to identify the precise molecular nodes where signals converge or diverge. The core innovation is the use of optogenetic receptor engineering to isolate and rewire specific cytoplasmic domains, allowing direct testing of which motifs are necessary and sufficient for differential transcriptional and phenotypic outcomes. This approach will define the fundamental logic—additive, synergistic, or antagonistic—by which these two distinct signal types are integrated in innate immune cells.

ideas.title: A high-fidelity in vivo atlas enables engineering of a physiologically matched in vitro B cell activation model
ideas.description: This project first establishes a definitive molecular atlas of human germinal center and memory B cell states by performing single-cell RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, and BCR sequencing on cells directly isolated from primary human lymphoid tissues. This high-resolution in vivo reference is then used as a quantitative benchmark to engineer and iteratively refine a complex in vitro culture system incorporating defined stromal cell types, antigen-presenting cells, and cytokine gradients. The success of the model is measured by its ability to generate blast cells that cluster indistinguishably from their in vivo counterparts across the multi-omic landscape, not just by superficial marker expression. This approach ensures the final in vitro model is validated against the true physiological standard, overcoming the circularity of comparing one artificial system to another.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Lineage Tracking of B Cell Fate Decisions Using Microfluidics and Time-Lapse Imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from developmental biology and stem cell research—the use of microfluidic devices for long-term, high-throughput lineage tracking of single progenitor cells—to the study of B cell fate decisions. The analogous problem of understanding how a single stem cell's environmental exposure history dictates its differentiation path is solved using microfluidic traps and time-lapse imaging. Adapted here, a custom microfluidic device will be used to trap hundreds of individual, primary B cells in separate chambers under continuous perfusion. Following isolation, each cell will be subjected to controlled, sequential activation pulses via RP105, CD40, and BCR ligation delivered through the microfluidic channels. Using time-lapse microscopy, the fate of each cell (e.g., division, death, differentiation marker expression via fluorescent reporters) will be tracked and correlated with its precise stimulation history and pre-fate behavior, enabling the construction of high-resolution causal maps from signal input to cellular output at the single-cell level.

ideas.title: Live-cell FRET biosensor imaging integrated with computational modeling to resolve the dynamic architecture of B-cell receptor co-signaling networks
ideas.description: This approach combines longitudinal, single-cell live imaging using a panel of FRET-based biosensors specific to key nodes downstream of RP105, CD40, and the BCR to capture the real-time dynamics of signaling activity. High-content imaging data provides continuous, multi-parameter kinetic traces from individual cells, enabling the inference of causal relationships and feedback loops within the network. These dynamic datasets are then used to parameterize and train computational models (e.g., ordinary differential equation or Boolean network models) that predict network behavior. The models are rigorously validated by comparing their predictions to signaling outcomes following sequential pharmacological inhibition or CRISPR-mediated knockout of specific network components.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Convergence Analysis from Neuroscience to Map Shared TLR/CD40 Signaling Hubs
ideas.description: This idea proposes that the RP105 and CD40 receptor systems, while initiating distinct signal types, may converge on shared intracellular signaling hubs, which are then modulated differently to produce specific cellular outcomes. Drawing an analogy from systems neuroscience, where the convergence and differential modulation of distinct sensory inputs on shared neural circuits is a solved problem through connectivity mapping and dynamic modeling. The adapted research idea applies this solution by using phospho-proteomic network analysis and computational modeling of B cell signaling dynamics. This approach will map the immediate signaling cascades from each receptor to identify convergent nodes (e.g., in NF-κB or MAPK pathways) and then model how variables like signal strength, temporal sequence, and spatial compartmentalization within the cell differentially modulate these shared hubs, explaining how similar pathways can drive divergent functional responses like proliferation, survival, or antibody class switching.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Sensor Arrays to Map B Cell Signaling Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from environmental science, where spatially distributed sensor arrays are used to model complex systems like ocean currents. The analogous solution involves deploying multiple sensors (e.g., for temperature, salinity) and integrating their simultaneous readings to create a predictive model of the system's behavior. Adapted to immunology, high-throughput phospho-specific flow cytometry will be used not just for parallel measurement, but as a coordinated 'signaling sensor array' for the key B cell receptors RP105, CD40, and BCR. The simultaneous, multi-parameter phospho-protein data from this array will be fed into multivariate analysis and network inference algorithms, similar to those used in environmental modeling. This approach will allow the construction of a predictive map of the B cell signaling network, revealing how signals from these receptors converge, interact, and produce integrated cellular responses under different stimulatory conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Voltage-Sensitive Dye Logic to FRET Biosensors for B Cell Activation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from neuroscience, where voltage-sensitive dyes image rapid, localized electrical signals in neuronal networks, we adapt this solution to B cell biology. The analogous problem of capturing fast, subcellular signaling events is solved by using reporters with fast kinetics and precise subcellular targeting. The adapted research idea is to engineer FRET-based biosensors with ultra-fast fluorescence switching and specific targeting to the B cell immunological synapse. This will visualize the real-time, nanoscale dynamics of key protein interactions (like BCR microcluster formation or intracellular kinase recruitment) immediately following antigen encounter, providing a spatial-temporal map of early activation events previously obscured by slower or globally targeted biosensors.
